teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the divine is immanent and can be perceived through inner vision cultivated by meditation. Akrura's vision in the Yamuna symbolizes the inner realization of the Lord's universal form.

chapter_summary: Akrura, upon seeing Krishna's divine form, is overwhelmed with devotion and offers a prayer. He describes Krishna's attributes, including the Śrīvatsa mark, Kaustubha gem, and garland, and mentions the attendants like Sunanda, Nanda, Sanaka, and others. Akrura praises Krishna with a choked voice, bowing his head and folding his hands.

story.title: Akrura's Vision and Prayer
story.arc: Krishna's divine revelation to Akrura
story.timeline_note: During Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Akrura's overwhelmed emotional state and the vivid description of Krishna's divine form and attendants.

teachings.teaching: The devotee's ecstatic love for the Lord is expressed through physical symptoms like standing of bodily hairs and tear-filled eyes.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the culmination of bhakti-yoga where the practitioner's entire being is absorbed in the divine, leading to spontaneous kriya manifestations.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is served by various divine potencies like Sri, Pusti, etc., indicating his supreme opulence.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the concept of 'siddhi' in Kriya-Yoga, where the practitioner realizes the Lord's internal energies as part of his own spiritual experience.

chapter_summary: Akrūra offers a profound prayer to Kṛṣṇa, acknowledging Him as the supreme cause of all causes, the source of the universe, and the ultimate object of all forms of worship. He describes the cosmic form of the Lord, enumerates His avatars, and confesses his own ignorance and bondage to material existence, seeking refuge at Kṛṣṇa's lotus feet.

story.title: Akrūra's Prayer to Kṛṣṇa
story.arc: Akrūra, having been sent by Kaṃsa to bring Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma to Mathurā, takes the opportunity to offer a heartfelt prayer acknowledging Kṛṣṇa's supremacy and seeking His grace.
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs after Akrūra meets Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma and before they enter Mathurā.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's prayer is a monologue; visually, it could be depicted as a devotional scene with Akrūra bowing before Kṛṣṇa, with cosmic imagery in the background.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate cause of all causes, and all elements, senses, and deities are His parts.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization that the entire universe is a manifestation of the Supreme Consciousness, and all actions are offerings to that Consciousness.

teachings.teaching: The unmanifest (ajā) and its qualities cannot know the Self; the Self is beyond the guṇas.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the Kriya-Yoga practitioner must transcend the guṇas through meditation to realize the Self.

teachings.teaching: All forms of worship, whether Vedic, yogic, or devotional, ultimately lead to the same Supreme Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga integrates all paths, recognizing that the goal is union with the Supreme, which is achieved through disciplined practice and devotion.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's cosmic form encompasses all elements, beings, and directions.
teachings.lens_note: This vision of the cosmic form is a meditative realization in Kriya-Yoga, where the practitioner sees the entire universe as the body of the Lord.

teachings.teaching: The Lord descends in various avatars for the protection of the righteous and destruction of the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma views avatars as manifestations of divine energy that guide practitioners on the path of Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: The conditioned soul is bewildered by māyā and identifies with the body and possessions, leading to suffering.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga aims to break this identification through meditation and self-inquiry, leading to liberation.

teachings.teaching: Surrender to the Lord is the ultimate means to overcome the senses and attain liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, surrender (prapatti) is the culmination of practice, where the practitioner offers all actions to the divine.

chapter_summary: Akrūra recounts his vision of the Lord in the Yamuna, then proceeds to Mathura with Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma. They enter the city, are welcomed by the citizens, and Kṛṣṇa kills the washerman, blesses the garland-maker Sudāman, and receives garlands.

story.title: Akrūra's Vision and Entry into Mathurā
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Mathurā
story.timeline_note: After the vision in the Yamunā, before the wrestling match with Kaṃsa's wrestlers.
story.comic_potential: The washerman's arrogant refusal and swift beheading; the women's frantic rush to see Kṛṣṇa.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's form is both immanent and transcendent; Akrūra sees the universe in Kṛṣṇa's form.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this vision represents the realization of the cosmic form (viśvarūpa) through deep meditation, where the practitioner sees the unity of all existence in the divine.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is equal to all beings and bestows grace on devotees regardless of status.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the divine presence is equally accessible to all who practice sincere devotion and meditation, transcending social hierarchies.

teachings.teaching: True wealth is devotion to the Lord; Sudāman asks for unwavering bhakti.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the highest goal is not material gain but unbroken devotion (bhakti) and compassion for all beings, which purifies the mind and leads to liberation.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the aftermath of Krishna's boon to Sudama, where Sudama returns home to find his hut transformed into a palace and his family blessed with wealth and prosperity. Krishna's grace is highlighted as the source of all material and spiritual fulfillment.

story.title: Sudama's Blessing
story.arc: Sudama's return and transformation
story.timeline_note: Immediately after Sudama's visit to Dwaraka
story.comic_potential: Sudama's astonishment at his transformed home

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Krishna brings both material and spiritual prosperity.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes that sincere devotion (bhakti) aligns one's actions with divine will, leading to natural fulfillment of desires without attachment.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama enter Mathura, encounter the hunchbacked woman Trivakra who offers them sandalwood paste, Krishna straightens her body, then they break the great bow in the arena, kill the guards, and spend the night in the city. Kamsa, hearing of these events, becomes terrified and organizes a wrestling festival the next day.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama in Mathura
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Mathura leading to the killing of Kamsa
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Putana and other demons in Vrindavan, Krishna and Balarama go to Mathura for the first time.
story.comic_potential: The straightening of Trivakra's hunchback and the breaking of the bow are visually striking and humorous.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's touch can transform and elevate even the most humble and deformed.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes how the practice of yoga (union with the divine) straightens the crooked tendencies of the mind and body, leading to spiritual beauty.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is not bound by material rules; He acts with playful spontaneity (lila).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that divine actions are not subject to karmic laws; they are expressions of freedom and grace.

teachings.teaching: Fear of death arises from ignorance of the self; Kamsa's omens show his attachment to the body.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that realization of the eternal self removes fear; Kamsa's nightmares reflect his identification with the perishable body.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama arrive at the wrestling arena, kill the elephant Kuvalayapida, enter the arena, and are challenged by the wrestlers Canura and Mustika. The crowd acclaims them as incarnations of Narayana.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama Enter the Wrestling Arena
story.arc: Kamsa's persecution and Krishna's childhood exploits
story.timeline_note: After the killing of various demons in Vrindavan, Krishna and Balarama go to Mathura for the wrestling festival.
story.comic_potential: High: Elephant fight, crowd reactions, dramatic entry.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's pastimes are performed with ease and playfulness (lila).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that divine actions are effortless and spontaneous, reflecting the state of sahaja (naturalness) in advanced practice.

teachings.teaching: One should act in accordance with time and place (desa-kala-ucita).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes adapting practice to one's circumstances; Krishna's response to Canura shows wisdom in choosing appropriate action.

teachings.teaching: The king represents all beings (sarva-bhuta-maya).
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga view of the guru as the embodiment of all beings, deserving respect and service.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama defeat and kill the wrestlers Canura, Mustika, Kuta, Sala, and Tosala in the arena of Kamsa. Kamsa orders the arrest of Nanda and Vasudeva, but Krishna leaps onto his platform, grabs him by the hair, and throws him to the ground, killing him. Kamsa's brothers attack but are slain by Balarama. The women of Kamsa's family lament. Krishna and Balarama then release their parents and bow to them.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama Kill Kamsa and His Wrestlers
story.arc: Kamsa's death and liberation of Devaki and Vasudeva
story.timeline_note: After the wrestling match, Krishna kills Kamsa on the spot.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic wrestling moves, divine feats, and the sudden leap onto the platform.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects the righteous and destroys the wicked; even a king who opposes God's will meets his end.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'Ishvara pranidhana' (surrender to God) and the inevitable dissolution of ego when it opposes the divine will.

teachings.teaching: One who harms innocent beings will not find peace; adharma leads to one's own destruction.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that actions (kriya) rooted in selfish desire (kama) create karmic bonds; Kamsa's cruelty exemplifies the suffering caused by ignorance of one's true nature.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the source and protector of all beings; those who disregard Him suffer.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga teaching that the inner Self (Atman) is the true controller; ignoring this leads to misery.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama return to Mathura, reunite with their parents Vasudeva and Devaki, and console them. They install Ugrasena as king, welcome back the Yadava clans who had fled due to Kamsa, and send Nanda and the cowherds back to Vrindavan with gifts. Krishna and Balarama undergo the sacred thread ceremony with Garga as teacher, then study under Sandipani in Avanti, learning all scriptures and arts in 64 days. As guru-dakshina, they retrieve Sandipani's son from the ocean, killing the demon Panchajana and recovering the conch, then fetching the son from Yama's city. The guru blesses them, and they return to Mathura.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama's Return to Mathura and Education
story.arc: Krishna's early life and education
story.timeline_note: After Kamsa's death, before Krishna's later exploits.
story.comic_potential: The guru-dakshina adventure: retrieving a dead son from the ocean and Yama's city, with a conch-blowing scene.

teachings.teaching: One must serve parents and elders; failing to do so leads to suffering after death.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of selfless service (seva) as a foundational practice for purifying the mind and preparing for higher yoga.

teachings.teaching: The guru is to be honored with devotion; the disciple should repay the guru's debt.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework highlights guru-bhakti as a key element of Kriya-Yoga, where the guru's grace is essential for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: Even the all-knowing Lord conceals his divine nature and acts like a human to teach humility.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the principle of lila (divine play) and the importance of humility in spiritual practice, as emphasized in Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: Uddhava is sent by Krishna to Vrindavan to console Nanda, Yashoda, and the gopis who are suffering from separation. Uddhava delivers Krishna's message, describing his divine nature and assuring them of his return. The chapter ends with the gopis' morning activities and their curiosity about Uddhava's arrival.

story.title: Uddhava's Visit to Vrindavan
story.arc: Krishna's separation and Uddhava's consolation
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura, before his return to Vrindavan.
story.comic_potential: The gopis' morning activities and their curiosity about Uddhava's chariot could be depicted humorously.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial and equal to all, without favorites or enemies.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the state of samatva (equanimity) where the practitioner sees the divine in all beings.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's incarnations are for the protection of the righteous and for pastime.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that divine play (lila) is a teaching tool to inspire devotion and detachment.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate cause and pervades all existence.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga understanding of the omnipresent consciousness that is realized through meditation.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to the Lord leads to liberation from karma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that focused devotion (bhakti) purifies the mind and accelerates spiritual progress.

chapter_summary: Uddhava visits the gopis of Vrindavan as a messenger from Krishna. The gopis express their intense longing and separation from Krishna, lamenting his absence and questioning his love. Uddhava consoles them with Krishna's message, which includes teachings on the nature of the self and the purpose of separation to deepen devotion.

story.title: The Gopis' Lament and Krishna's Message
story.arc: Separation and Devotion
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura, Uddhava visits Vrindavan.
story.comic_potential: The gopis' dramatic expressions of love and longing, and Uddhava's role as a messenger, offer rich emotional and humorous contrasts.

teachings.teaching: The self (atma) is pure consciousness, distinct from the body and mind, and is unaffected by the three states of waking, dreaming, and deep sleep.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the practice of witnessing the mind's modifications and realizing the seer as separate.

teachings.teaching: One should control the senses by withdrawing the mind from sense objects, like one who is awake controls dreams.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga practice of pratyahara (sense withdrawal) to still the mind.

teachings.teaching: All spiritual practices (yoga, sankhya, renunciation, austerity, etc.) culminate in knowledge of the self.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga integrates these paths through direct inner experience.

teachings.teaching: Separation from the beloved (Krishna) intensifies devotion and is a means to deepen the mind's connection with him.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the pain of separation (viraha) is used to turn the mind inward and focus on the divine presence within.

teachings.teaching: The highest happiness is in detachment (nairashya), as taught by the prostitute Pingala.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework values inner contentment over external desires; detachment is a key step in Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: Uddhava visits Vrindavan to console the gopis and Nanda's family after Krishna's departure. He stays for several months, singing Krishna's pastimes and alleviating their grief. The gopis' intense love for Krishna is praised as the highest devotion. Uddhava returns to Mathura and reports their bhakti to Krishna.

story.title: Uddhava's Consolation of the Gopis
story.arc: Krishna's departure from Vrindavan and the aftermath
story.timeline_note: After Krishna leaves Vrindavan for Mathura, Uddhava is sent to console the residents.
story.comic_potential: Uddhava's attempt to philosophize while the gopis are in intense separation; the contrast between intellectual devotion and emotional bhakti.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' love for Krishna is the highest form of devotion, surpassing even that of great yogis and sages.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the pinnacle of bhakti where the devotee's entire being is absorbed in the divine, transcending all practices.

teachings.teaching: Even those who are seemingly unqualified (like the gopis) can attain the highest spiritual perfection through pure love for God.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga is accessible to all, regardless of external purity; inner devotion is key.

teachings.teaching: The dust of the feet of the gopis is supremely purifying, as their songs about Krishna purify the three worlds.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga principle of satsang and the power of divine association.

teachings.teaching: The residents of Vrindavan pray that their mind, speech, and body be always engaged in Krishna's service.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches the integration of all faculties in devotion, aligning with this prayer.

chapter_summary: Krishna visits the courtesan's house, grants her a boon, then visits Akrura. Akrura praises Krishna and is sent to Hastinapura to check on the Pandavas.

story.title: Krishna's Visit to Akrura and the Courtesan
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Kamsa, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's playful visit to the courtesan and his granting of a boon, followed by the philosophical discourse with Akrura.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the supreme controller and the cause of all creation, maintenance, and destruction.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the ultimate reality as the source of all action, and the practitioner should surrender to that supreme will.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to protect dharma and destroy the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga view that divine descent occurs to restore balance and guide practitioners on the path.

teachings.teaching: Saints purify by their mere sight, unlike holy waters and deities made of material elements.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the company of realized souls is considered more powerful than external rituals for inner purification.

teachings.teaching: One should not be attached to material possessions and family; the Lord's maya binds the soul.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches detachment through inner discipline and devotion to cut the knots of material bondage.

chapter_summary: Akrūra visits Hastinapura, meets with Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Bhīṣma, Vidura, Kuntī, and others. Kuntī laments her condition and prays to Kṛṣṇa. Akrūra advises Dhṛtarāṣṭra to treat the Pāṇḍavas equally, but Dhṛtarāṣṭra admits his attachment to his sons. Akrūra returns to Dvārakā and reports to Rāma and Kṛṣṇa.

story.title: Akrūra's Mission to Hastinapura
story.arc: Pre-Kurukshetra diplomacy
story.timeline_note: After the Pāṇḍavas' exile, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's diplomatic visit, Kuntī's emotional plea, Dhṛtarāṣṭra's inner conflict.

teachings.teaching: One is born alone, dies alone, and experiences the results of one's actions alone.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the solitary nature of spiritual practice; each individual must perform their own sadhana.

teachings.teaching: Wealth acquired by adharma is taken away by others; one who accumulates it is foolish.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's lens: Kriya-Yoga purifies the mind; adharmic actions create karmic debts that bind the soul.

teachings.teaching: The world is like a dream, illusion, and mental construct; one should see it as such and become peaceful.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that through meditation, one realizes the world is a projection of the mind; detachment leads to peace.

teachings.teaching: No one can change the will of the Lord; He descends to relieve the earth's burden.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's lens: The Lord's descent (avatara) is a cosmic event; Kriya-Yoga aligns the practitioner with divine will.

chapter_summary: Jarāsandha, king of Magadha, attacks Mathurā with a vast army to avenge his son-in-law Kaṃsa's death. Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, understanding their divine purpose to relieve the earth's burden, engage in battle. Kṛṣṇa decimates the enemy forces, while Balarāma captures Jarāsandha but spares him at Kṛṣṇa's request. Jarāsandha returns to Magadha. Later, the Yavana king Kālayavana, instigated by Nārada, besieges Mathurā. Kṛṣṇa builds a fortress in the sea (Dvārakā) to protect the Yadus.

story.title: Jarāsandha's Attack and the Building of Dvārakā
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's early life and establishment of Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: After Kaṃsa's death, Jarāsandha attacks Mathurā seventeen times; on the eighteenth, Kālayavana appears.
story.comic_potential: High: epic battles, divine weapons, strategic retreat, and city-building.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to relieve the earth's burden and protect the righteous.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of dharma-rakṣaṇa: the divine descends to restore cosmic balance when adharma prevails.

teachings.teaching: A true hero does not boast but demonstrates prowess through action.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes kriya (action) over vāda (words); Kṛṣṇa's response to Jarāsandha's taunt exemplifies this.

teachings.teaching: Strategic withdrawal is not cowardice but wisdom to protect one's dependents.
teachings.lens_note: Kṛṣṇa's decision to build Dvārakā illustrates viveka (discernment) in Kriya-Yoga: acting with foresight to preserve the sādhaka's environment.

chapter_summary: Krishna builds Dvaraka with divine gifts from the gods, including Sudharma assembly hall and Parijata tree from Indra, swift horses from Varuna, and treasures from Kubera. He then leaves the city unarmed, accompanied by Balarama, to meet the citizens.

story.title: Building Dvaraka and Divine Gifts
story.arc: Krishna establishes his capital with divine opulence and then goes out to meet the people.
story.timeline_note: After Krishna settled in Dvaraka, the gods offered their gifts.
story.comic_potential: Krishna leaving the city unarmed while wearing a garland could be depicted as a serene yet powerful moment.

teachings.teaching: The divine gifts from the gods symbolize that a devotee of the Lord receives all opulences without effort.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that when one is established in the self (atma), the universe naturally offers its treasures.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's unarmed departure shows that true strength lies in spiritual power, not weapons.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the yogi's ability to act without attachment, relying on inner stillness.

chapter_summary: Mucukunda, a great king from the Ikshvaku dynasty, had been guarding the gods in a cave for ages. After the Yavana king is burned by Mucukunda's angry glance, Lord Krishna reveals Himself to Mucukunda, who recognizes Him as Narayana and offers prayers of surrender, realizing the futility of material pursuits.

story.title: Mucukunda's Liberation
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes of delivering the surrendered souls
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Kamsa and other demons, during Krishna's sojourn in Mathura.
story.comic_potential: Mucukunda's long sleep and the Yavana's mistaken kick, followed by his incineration, has slapstick humor.

teachings.teaching: The material world is full of misery; one should not be attached to family, wealth, and kingdom.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes detachment from sensory objects as a prerequisite for inner stillness. Mucukunda's regret mirrors the practitioner's need to withdraw from external pursuits.

teachings.teaching: Time is all-powerful and devours everything; only surrender to the Lord grants true peace.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the awareness of time's relentless flow motivates the practitioner to seek the eternal Self within, beyond the temporal body.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the only one who can grant liberation; even the gods cannot give kaivalya.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework stresses that ultimate freedom (kaivalya) comes only through divine grace, not through any external means.

chapter_summary: King Mucukunda, after being awakened by Kṛṣṇa, recounts his past life as a powerful king who conquered the world but ultimately found no lasting happiness. He describes the futility of material pursuits and the inevitability of death. He then expresses his desire only for devotion to Kṛṣṇa, rejecting all boons. Kṛṣṇa praises his steadfastness, explains that even yogic practices without devotion are insufficient, and grants him unalloyed devotion. He instructs Mucukunda to continue his kingly duties while remaining absorbed in Him, and promises that in his next life he will become a brāhmaṇa and attain Him.

story.title: Mucukunda's Liberation
story.arc: Mucukunda, after being awakened by Kṛṣṇa, reflects on the vanity of worldly power and seeks only devotion. Kṛṣṇa praises his detachment and grants him unalloyed devotion, promising future liberation.
story.timeline_note: After Kṛṣṇa killed Kālayavana, Mucukunda was awakened from his long sleep. This chapter is his discourse on renunciation and Kṛṣṇa's response.
story.comic_potential: Mucukunda's vivid description of the king's body turning into feces, worms, and ashes, and his rejection of all boons, could be depicted with dramatic irony.

teachings.teaching: Material achievements and sensual pleasures are temporary and ultimately lead to suffering. The body decays into filth, worms, and ashes.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this highlights the impermanence of the physical body and the need to transcend identification with it through inner practice.

teachings.teaching: Even asceticism and yoga without devotion are insufficient; desires remain latent and resurface.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga must be coupled with bhakti to purify the vāsanās; otherwise, the mind remains restless.

teachings.teaching: True liberation comes from association with saints and unwavering devotion to the Supreme Lord.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's view that sat-saṅga and surrender to the guru are essential for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: One should perform worldly duties with mind fixed on God, and thus burn past sins.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that action done without attachment and with devotion becomes a means of purification.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of Rukmiṇī's marriage to Kṛṣṇa. After the demon Mura is slain, Kṛṣṇa returns to Dvārakā. Meanwhile, King Bhīṣmaka of Vidarbha has a daughter Rukmiṇī, who desires to marry Kṛṣṇa. Her brother Rukmī opposes this and arranges her marriage to Śiśupāla. Rukmiṇī sends a brāhmaṇa messenger to Kṛṣṇa with a secret message, pleading with him to abduct her before the wedding. Kṛṣṇa agrees and prepares to rescue her.

story.title: The Abduction of Rukmiṇī
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's marriages and pastimes
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Mura and before the great war
story.comic_potential: High: secret love letters, daring abduction, battle with rivals

teachings.teaching: Contentment is the highest wealth; even a poor contented man sleeps happily, while a discontented king suffers.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, contentment (santoṣa) is a key niyama; it stabilizes the mind for deeper meditation.

teachings.teaching: The brāhmaṇa who is content with his lot and devoted to dharma is worthy of worship.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that a true brāhmaṇa is one who lives by spiritual values, not birth; such a person is a natural guru.

teachings.teaching: A king who protects his subjects and ensures their happiness is dear to the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of selfless service (karma-yoga) as a path to divine connection.

teachings.teaching: Rukmiṇī's devotion: she considers Kṛṣṇa as her only refuge and is willing to give up her life if she cannot attain his grace.
teachings.lens_note: This exemplifies śaraṇāgati (surrender), a core attitude in Kriya-Yoga for dissolving the ego.

chapter_summary: Krishna receives Rukmini's message, confesses his mutual love, and decides to abduct her before her forced marriage to Shishupala. He travels to Kundina with a brahmana messenger. Meanwhile, Rukmini's father prepares for the wedding, and many enemy kings assemble to prevent Krishna. Balarama follows with an army. Rukmini anxiously awaits Krishna, and the brahmana returns with good news. The townspeople rejoice, and Rukmini goes to the temple of Ambika (Parvati) to pray. After worship, she leaves the temple.

relationships.rel: plans to give in marriage to

story.title: Rukmini's Abduction Plan
story.arc: Krishna's marriage to Rukmini
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Rukmini sends a message to Krishna and before the actual abduction.
story.comic_potential: High: romantic tension, secret messages, anxious waiting, dramatic arrival of kings, and a temple scene with prayers.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's reciprocation of love: He admits he cannot sleep thinking of Rukmini.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'prema-bhakti' where the divine responds to the devotee's longing with equal intensity, catalyzing spiritual union.

teachings.teaching: Rukmini's prayer to Ambika: She prays for Krishna as her husband.
teachings.lens_note: This exemplifies 'śaraṇāgati' (surrender) and the use of devotional prayer as a kriya to align one's will with the divine, a key practice in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: The townspeople's spontaneous praise: They declare Rukmini deserves Krishna and vice versa.
teachings.lens_note: Reflects the Kriya-Yoga concept of 'satsanga' (holy company) where the community's collective devotion reinforces individual spiritual progress.

chapter_summary: Rukmiṇī, adorned with jewels, walks gracefully to the temple, captivating the kings. She boards Krishna's chariot, and Krishna abducts her before the assembled kings, who are humiliated.

story.title: The Abduction of Rukmiṇī
story.arc: Krishna's marriage to Rukmiṇī
story.timeline_note: After Rukmiṇī's letter to Krishna, she goes to the temple and is abducted.
story.comic_potential: Rukmiṇī's graceful walk and the kings' infatuation, followed by their shock at the abduction.

teachings.teaching: Divine beauty can captivate even the most powerful, but the Lord's will prevails.
teachings.lens_note: The kings are mesmerized by Rukmiṇī's beauty, yet Krishna effortlessly takes her.

chapter_summary: The Yadava army, led by Krishna and Balarama, defeats the assembled kings. Rukmi, Rukmini's brother, pursues Krishna in anger, is defeated and humiliated but spared at Rukmini's plea. Balarama then teaches Rukmini about the nature of the self and detachment.

story.title: The Defeat of Rukmi and Balarama's Teaching
story.arc: Krishna's marriage to Rukmini and subsequent conflicts
story.timeline_note: Immediately after Krishna's abduction of Rukmini from her svayamvara.
story.comic_potential: Rukmi's boastful challenge and subsequent humiliation, including having his head shaved, has comic elements.

teachings.teaching: The self (atman) is distinct from the body, which is subject to birth and death. Attachment to the body causes suffering.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes discerning the self from the body-mind complex through meditative practices. This teaching directly supports that discernment.

teachings.teaching: One should not grieve over the inevitable; the wise understand the transient nature of material things.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, equanimity is cultivated through inner stillness. This teaching encourages detachment from outcomes.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate controller; all events are under divine will.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees surrender to the divine as key to transcending ego. This teaching reinforces that perspective.

chapter_summary: Rukmiṇī's abduction by Kṛṣṇa is celebrated in Dvārakā with great festivity. Rukmī, defeated and humiliated, builds the city of Bhojakaṭa and vows not to enter Kuṇḍina until he kills Kṛṣṇa. The marriage of Kṛṣṇa and Rukmiṇī is performed, and the Yadus rejoice.

story.title: Rukmiṇī's Abduction and Marriage
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa abducts Rukmiṇī, defeats Rukmī, and marries her in Dvārakā with great celebration.
story.timeline_note: After the abduction, Rukmī builds Bhojakaṭa and vows revenge; the marriage occurs immediately.
story.comic_potential: The contrast between Rukmī's furious vow and the joyous wedding festivities offers dramatic irony.

teachings.teaching: One should abandon mental distress and fix the mind on the self (buddhyā samādadhe).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this indicates the practice of pratyāhāra (withdrawal) and dhāraṇā (concentration) to stabilize the mind.

teachings.teaching: The Lord marries according to Vedic rites, showing the importance of dharma in relationships.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that even divine actions follow śāstra, teaching that spiritual life includes righteous conduct.

teachings.teaching: Devotees experience unique joy in Kṛṣṇa's presence, as seen in Dvārakā's celebration.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga goal of ānanda (bliss) through union with the divine.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of Pradyumna, the son of Krishna and Rukmini, who is an incarnation of Kamadeva. He is stolen as an infant by the demon Shambara, thrown into the ocean, swallowed by a fish, and eventually recovered. Raised by Mayavati (Rati in disguise), he learns his true identity, defeats and kills Shambara, and returns to Dvaraka, bringing joy to his family.

story.title: The Return of Pradyumna
story.arc: Pradyumna's abduction, survival, and triumphant return to Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's marriage to Rukmini and before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: High: infant swallowed by fish, raised by demoness, identity reveal, epic battle with mace and magic, joyful reunion.

teachings.teaching: The divine play (lila) of the Lord includes apparent loss and recovery, demonstrating that nothing is ever truly lost in the spiritual realm.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of pratyahara (withdrawal of senses) and the eventual return to the source. Pradyumna's journey symbolizes the soul's temporary forgetfulness and its awakening to its true nature through divine grace.

teachings.teaching: Even in the face of great adversity, one's true identity and purpose will be revealed through divine intervention and guidance.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on the guru's role in awakening the disciple. Narada's revelation to Mayavati and later to Rukmini mirrors the guru's function of dispelling ignorance.

teachings.teaching: The power of divine knowledge (vidya) overcomes all illusions (maya).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that the practice of meditation and mantra (vidya) destroys the veils of maya. Mayavati's gift of the Mahamaya vidya to Pradyumna symbolizes the transmission of spiritual knowledge that enables one to conquer delusion.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of the Syamantaka jewel. Satrajit, a devotee of the Sun god, receives the jewel from Surya. His brother Prasena is killed by a lion while wearing it, and the lion is killed by Jambavan. Krishna is falsely accused of taking the jewel, so he tracks down the truth, fights Jambavan for 28 days, and retrieves the jewel. He returns it to Satrajit, who then gives his daughter Satyabhama in marriage to Krishna along with the jewel, but Krishna declines the jewel.

story.title: The Syamantaka Jewel
story.arc: Krishna clears his name and marries Satyabhama and Jambavati
story.timeline_note: Occurs during Krishna's residence in Dvaraka, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: The mistaken identity and the 28-day wrestling match between Krishna and Jambavan have humorous elements.

teachings.teaching: False accusations can be cleared by seeking the truth.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the purification of the mind through diligent inquiry (jijnasa) to remove misconceptions.

teachings.teaching: One should not be attached to material wealth; even a jewel can cause trouble.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes detachment (vairagya) as essential for yoga; the jewel symbolizes worldly desires that lead to conflict.

teachings.teaching: The Lord accepts the devotee's offering but does not take the material gift.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the principle of offering actions to God without expecting personal gain, a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga karma yoga.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama visit Hastinapura to console the Pandavas and Kunti after the fire incident. Meanwhile, Satrajit is murdered by Shatadhanva for the Syamantaka jewel. Shatadhanva flees, pursued by Krishna and Balarama. Krishna kills Shatadhanva but the jewel is missing. Balarama suspects Krishna and goes to Mithila. Later, Krishna retrieves the jewel from Akrura, who had hidden it. The chapter ends with the glorification of the Syamantaka story.

story.title: The Syamantaka Jewel and the Death of Shatadhanva
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After the Pandavas' fire incident, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: High; chase scene, jewel theft, mistaken suspicion, and reconciliation.

teachings.teaching: Krishna, though omniscient, acts according to social norms to console the afflicted.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'lila' or divine play, where the supreme being engages in human-like actions to teach dharma.

teachings.teaching: Greed leads to destruction; Shatadhanva's greed for the jewel caused his death.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that attachment to material wealth (kama) binds one to samsara; detachment is essential for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: One should not harbor suspicion without evidence; Balarama's suspicion of Krishna was unfounded.
teachings.lens_note: This teaches the importance of faith (shraddha) in the guru and divine, a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: The Syamantaka jewel symbolizes divine grace; it brings prosperity to the virtuous and disaster to the unworthy.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the jewel represents the spiritual treasure (atma-jnana) that only the pure-hearted can possess.

teachings.teaching: Hearing and reciting the story of the Syamantaka jewel removes sins and brings peace.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the practice of 'sravana' and 'kirtana' as means to purify the mind and attain liberation.

chapter_summary: Krishna visits the Pandavas in Indraprastha, is warmly received, and stays for several months. He and Arjuna go hunting, meet Kālindī (daughter of the Sun god) who desires to marry Krishna. Krishna marries her, and also marries Mitravindā, and Satyā (Nāgnajitī) after subduing seven fierce bulls. The chapter also recounts the burning of Khāṇḍava forest and the gifts from Agni and Maya.

story.title: Krishna's Marriages and the Burning of Khāṇḍava Forest
story.arc: Krishna's visit to Indraprastha and his marriages to Kālindī, Mitravindā, and Satyā, with flashbacks to the Khāṇḍava forest episode.
story.timeline_note: The chapter covers Krishna's stay in Indraprastha for several months, including a hunting trip, meeting Kālindī, and subsequent marriages. The Khāṇḍava forest burning is a past event referenced.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's playful subduing of the seven bulls and his multiple marriages provide opportunities for visual humor and action.

teachings.teaching: Krishna is the supreme lord who removes the suffering of his devotees and is the shelter of the helpless.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of surrender (prapatti) and the realization that the divine is the inner controller who removes obstacles when one is established in yoga.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is self-satisfied and does not need anything, yet he accepts the service of his devotees out of love.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the yogi should act without personal desire, mirroring Krishna's acceptance of the marriage proposal not out of need but to fulfill the devotee's wish.

teachings.teaching: One should not be proud of one's strength; the Lord easily subdues the arrogant.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga teaching of humility and the recognition that all power comes from the divine; the ego must be subdued through practice.

chapter_summary: Krishna marries many wives, including Satya (daughter of Nagnajit), Bhadra (daughter of Srutakirti), and Lakshmana (daughter of the Madra king). He also rescues many women from the demon Bhauma and marries them.

story.title: Krishna's Marriages
story.arc: Krishna marries multiple wives, demonstrating his divine pastimes and establishing alliances.
story.timeline_note: After the defeat of Bhauma and rescue of captive women.
story.comic_potential: The marriage processions and battles with kings provide dramatic and humorous scenes.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's marriages illustrate his role as the supreme enjoyer and protector of devotees.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this shows the integration of worldly duties (dharma) with divine love (prema), where every action is an offering.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna, accompanied by Satyabhama, travels to the city of Pragjyotisha to kill the demon Naraka (Bhauma). He destroys the fortifications, defeats Mura and his seven sons, and finally beheads Naraka. The Earth goddess (Bhumi) offers Naraka's earrings and other treasures to Krishna, who then frees 16,000 captive princesses, marries them, and returns to Dvaraka with the Parijata tree.

story.title: The Killing of Naraka and the Liberation of the Princesses
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's marriage to Satyabhama and before the Syamantaka episode.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic battle scenes, divine weapons, miraculous rescues, and a mass wedding.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects his devotees and destroys demons who oppress the world.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the inner purification where the divine will (Krishna) annihilates the ego's attachments (Naraka) and obstacles (Mura).

teachings.teaching: Surrender to the Lord (prapatti) brings liberation from fear and sin.
teachings.lens_note: Bhumi's prayer exemplifies the devotee's surrender; in Kriya-Yoga, this is the state of complete reliance on the divine, leading to inner peace.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate reality beyond all material distinctions.
teachings.lens_note: Bhumi's hymn (verses 25-30) describes Krishna as the supreme cause; in Kriya-Yoga, this is the realization of the Self as the non-dual consciousness.

teachings.teaching: The Lord marries his devotees, symbolizing the union of the individual soul with the divine.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the marriage of Krishna with the 16,000 princesses represents the integration of all aspects of the mind (princesses) with the supreme consciousness (Krishna).

chapter_summary: Krishna teases Rukmini about her choice of him as husband, pretending to be unworthy and suggesting she marry a suitable king. Rukmini, deeply hurt, faints. Krishna then consoles her, explaining his words were a jest to hear her loving speech. Rukmini responds with a profound discourse on her devotion, rejecting material kings and affirming Krishna as the supreme goal. Krishna praises her devotion and grants her blessings.

story.title: Krishna's Teasing and Rukmini's Devotion
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini, early in their household life.
story.comic_potential: High: Krishna's mock self-deprecation and Rukmini's dramatic fainting and subsequent eloquent rebuttal create a playful yet profound marital dialogue.

teachings.teaching: The supreme Lord is self-sufficient and not attached to material things; he is the goal of all desires.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this echoes the principle of 'Ishvara-pranidhana' (surrender to the Lord) as the highest practice, where the devotee recognizes the Lord as the sole refuge beyond material pursuits.

teachings.teaching: True love and marriage are based on spiritual equality, not material status.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes 'sattva' (purity) in relationships; this teaching aligns with the idea that union with the divine transcends social and material disparities.

teachings.teaching: The devotee should not be swayed by external appearances or worldly kings; only the Lord is worthy of devotion.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga focus on inner realization over outer ritual; Rukmini's rejection of material kings symbolizes the renunciation of ego and worldly attachments.

teachings.teaching: The body is impure and temporary; only devotion to the Lord's lotus feet is eternal.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary often highlights the need to transcend bodily identification; this verse directly supports the practice of 'dhyana' (meditation) on the divine form.

teachings.teaching: Those who seek material benefits from the Lord are misguided; the true devotee desires only the Lord himself.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this is the distinction between 'kamya' (desire-driven) and 'nishkama' (desireless) action; pure devotion is the goal.

chapter_summary: Rukmini expresses her deep love and devotion to Krishna, recounting how she chose him over other kings and how he rescued her. Krishna responds with playful and affectionate words, and the chapter concludes with their intimate conversation, illustrating divine love in human form.

story.title: Rukmini's Loving Lament and Krishna's Playful Response
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes as a householder
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini, during their early married life.
story.comic_potential: The playful banter and affectionate teasing between divine lovers, with Rukmini's mock anger and Krishna's charming replies.

teachings.teaching: The intimate relationship between the devotee and the divine is characterized by loving exchanges that transcend worldly norms.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the stage of 'sakhya-bhava' or friendly devotion, where the devotee interacts with the divine as an equal, fostering deep personal connection.

teachings.teaching: The divine, though self-sufficient, engages in human-like pastimes to teach the path of devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Krishna's 'human' actions are a deliberate teaching method, showing that the ultimate reality can be approached through personal love and service.

chapter_summary: This chapter lists the sons of Krishna by his chief queens, describes the marriage of Aniruddha to Rocana (Rukmi's daughter), and narrates the dice game between Balarama and Rukmi, which ends with Balarama killing Rukmi after insults.

story.title: The Sons of Krishna and the Dice Game of Balarama
story.arc: Genealogy and conflict resolution
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriages, his sons are born; later, Aniruddha marries Rocana, and Balarama kills Rukmi during a dice game.
story.comic_potential: The dice game and Balarama's wrath, including knocking out Kalinga's teeth, have vivid visual potential.

teachings.teaching: The queens' devotion to Krishna, despite his impartiality, illustrates surrender to the divine.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this devotion corresponds to the practice of Ishvara Pranidhana, surrendering the ego to the inner Self.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's indifference to the queens' attempts to captivate him shows the yogi's detachment from sensory allure.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes inner stillness; the senses cannot disturb the realized yogi.

teachings.teaching: Balarama's anger and violence, though seemingly unyogic, demonstrate righteous indignation against dishonesty.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga allows for action in alignment with dharma; anger can be a tool when free from personal ego.

chapter_summary: King Parikshit asks Shuka about the marriage of Aniruddha to Usha, daughter of Bana, and the ensuing battle between Krishna and Shiva. Shuka narrates Bana's boon from Shiva, his pride, Usha's dream of Aniruddha, Citralekha's help in bringing Aniruddha to Usha, Bana's discovery, and Aniruddha's capture by Bana's serpent bonds.

story.title: The Marriage of Aniruddha and Usha and the Battle with Bana
story.arc: Aniruddha, grandson of Krishna, is brought by Citralekha to Usha, daughter of Bana. Bana discovers them and captures Aniruddha, leading to a future battle between Krishna and Shiva.
story.timeline_note: This story occurs after the Vamana incarnation and before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: High: romantic dream, secret love, magical transport, prideful demon, divine battle.

teachings.teaching: Pride leads to downfall; Bana's pride in his thousand arms leads to his defeat.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, pride is a form of ego that obstructs spiritual progress. Bana's arrogance is a klesha that must be overcome.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to God (Shiva) can grant boons, but misuse leads to suffering.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that devotion must be coupled with humility and wisdom; otherwise, boons become traps.

teachings.teaching: Divine love transcends social boundaries; Usha's love for Aniruddha is predestined.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, true love is a reflection of the soul's longing for the Divine, which overcomes all obstacles.

chapter_summary: Bāṇa, a demon with a thousand arms, imprisons Aniruddha. The Vṛṣṇis, led by Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, attack Śoṇitapura. Śiva fights on Bāṇa's side. After a fierce battle, Kṛṣṇa cuts off Bāṇa's arms but spares his life due to Śiva's plea and a boon to Prahlāda. Bāṇa surrenders and releases Aniruddha.

story.title: The Battle with Bāṇa and the Rescue of Aniruddha
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Dvāpara Yuga
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Aniruddha's capture by Bāṇa; four months pass before the Vṛṣṇis learn of it via Nārada.
story.comic_potential: High: cosmic battle with gods, demons, magical weapons, a fever personified, and a dramatic surrender.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the supreme controller and the ultimate refuge; even Śiva and other gods bow to Him.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes surrendering the ego (ahamkara) to the divine will, recognizing that all power flows from the one source.

teachings.teaching: Material existence is like drowning in an ocean of misery due to attachment to family and possessions.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches detachment (vairagya) through inner discipline; the verse warns against being 'mohita-dhiya' (deluded intellect) which Kriya-Yoga aims to purify.

teachings.teaching: One who neglects the Lord's feet despite obtaining a human birth is pitiable and cheats himself.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga stresses the rare opportunity of human birth for self-realization; neglecting it is the greatest folly.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's mercy is available to all, even demons, if they surrender.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle that grace (prasada) is accessible through sincere practice and devotion, regardless of past karma.

chapter_summary: After being blessed by Lord Shiva, Lord Krishna returns to his capital Dvaraka with his wives. The city is beautifully decorated, and he enters to the sound of conches and drums, greeted by citizens, friends, and brahmanas. The chapter concludes by stating that one who recites this account of Krishna's victory over Shiva in the morning will never face defeat.

story.title: Krishna's Return to Dvaraka After Defeating Shiva
story.arc: Krishna's victory over Shiva and return to his capital
story.timeline_note: After the battle with Shiva described in previous chapters
story.comic_potential: Triumphant entry into a decorated city with fanfare

teachings.teaching: Reciting the account of Krishna's victory over Shiva in the morning ensures one will never face defeat.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes the power of daily remembrance (smriti) of divine victories to cultivate inner strength and overcome obstacles. The morning recitation aligns with the practice of setting a positive intention for the day.

chapter_summary: King Nṛga, a former king of the Ikṣvāku dynasty, recounts his fall from grace due to a mistaken donation of a cow that belonged to a brāhmaṇa. Despite his immense charity, he was cursed to become a lizard for eons until Kṛṣṇa liberated him. Kṛṣṇa then teaches the severe consequences of misappropriating brāhmaṇa property.

story.title: The Liberation of King Nṛga
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: Occurs during Kṛṣṇa's residence in Dvārakā, after the defeat of Bāṇāsura (Chapter 63).
story.comic_potential: The scene of Yadu boys trying to rescue a giant lizard from a dry well, followed by Kṛṣṇa effortlessly lifting it, has visual humor. The flashback of Nṛga's dilemma between two brāhmaṇas is dramatic.

teachings.teaching: Misappropriation of brāhmaṇa property, even unknowingly, leads to severe karmic consequences.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this underscores the principle of karma and the importance of purity in action (kriya). Even unintentional errors in dharma can have profound effects, emphasizing the need for vigilant self-awareness.

teachings.teaching: One should never harm a brāhmaṇa, even if he is at fault; instead, offer respect.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this as a call to transcend ego and cultivate humility, recognizing the divine in all beings, especially those who embody spiritual knowledge.

chapter_summary: Balarama visits Nanda's Gokula, is warmly received by the gopas and gopis who inquire about Krishna. He consoles them with Krishna's messages, stays for two months, and enjoys pastimes including drinking madhu from a tree hollow, summoning the Yamuna river, and dragging her with his plow when she disobeys. The river submits and he releases her, then sports in the water with the gopis.

story.title: Balarama's Visit to Gokula and the Yamuna's Submission
story.arc: Balarama's pastimes in Vraja
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's return to Mathura, Balarama visits Gokula; occurs during the rainy season (Madhu and Madhava months).
story.comic_potential: Balarama's drunken state, dragging the river with his plow, and the river's terrified plea offer vivid visual comedy.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' intense love for Krishna leads them to abandon all worldly attachments, yet they feel abandoned by him.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the stage of viraha-bhakti where the practitioner feels separation from the divine, purifying the heart.

teachings.teaching: Balarama's anger and subsequent mercy towards Yamuna shows the Lord's control over nature and his readiness to forgive the repentant.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that divine anger is a teaching tool; the devotee must surrender (prapatti) to receive grace.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' constant remembrance of Krishna's pastimes (smriti) sustains them in separation.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on smarana (remembrance) as a core practice to maintain connection with the divine.

chapter_summary: Pauṇḍraka, a foolish king, claims to be Vāsudeva and sends a messenger to Kṛṣṇa demanding that Kṛṣṇa surrender his insignia. Kṛṣṇa laughs, warns the messenger, and then goes to battle. Kṛṣṇa kills Pauṇḍraka and his ally the king of Kāśī. The king of Kāśī's son Sudakṣiṇa performs a black magic ritual to summon a fiery demon, which attacks Dvārakā. Kṛṣṇa sends his Sudarśana cakra, which destroys the demon and burns down Vārāṇasī. The chapter ends with a statement that hearing this story purifies one of sins.

story.title: The Killing of Pauṇḍraka and the Burning of Vārāṇasī
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes as the supreme lord, demonstrating his power and protecting his devotees.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Kṛṣṇa's return to Dvārakā from various pastimes.
story.comic_potential: High: Pauṇḍraka's foolish impersonation, Kṛṣṇa's laughter, the fiery demon, and the cakra's destruction provide vivid imagery.

teachings.teaching: False pride and imitation of the divine lead to destruction.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the danger of ego (ahamkara) claiming identity with the Self, which must be surrendered.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects his devotees and destroys evil forces.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the divine power (cakra) is the inner will that cuts through ignorance.

teachings.teaching: Hearing about the Lord's glories purifies one of sins.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the practice of śravaṇa (hearing) as a means of purification in Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about Balarāma's extraordinary deeds. Śuka narrates the story of Dvivida, a monkey friend of Narakāsura, who wreaks havoc in the Yadu kingdom to avenge his friend. He disrupts sages' hermitages, kidnaps people, and finally confronts Balarāma on Raivataka hill. After a fierce battle, Balarāma kills Dvivida with his fist, restoring peace.

story.title: The Slaying of Dvivida
story.arc: Balarāma's pastimes
story.timeline_note: After Narakāsura's death, Dvivida seeks revenge.
story.comic_potential: Dvivida's antics, including showing his buttocks to the women, and the playful yet fierce battle with Balarāma.

teachings.teaching: The divine protects the righteous and destroys the wicked, even when the wicked are powerful and cause widespread havoc.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'dharma-rakṣaṇa' — the maintenance of cosmic order through divine intervention, which corresponds to the yogi's inner purification where negative tendencies are uprooted by the power of awareness.

teachings.teaching: One should not be attached to revenge or anger, as it leads to one's own destruction.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes 'vairāgya' (detachment) and 'abhyāsa' (practice). Dvivida's attachment to revenge (rāga) and anger (krodha) leads to his downfall, mirroring how attachment to mental impurities hinders spiritual progress.

chapter_summary: Samba, son of Krishna and Jambavati, abducts Lakshmana, daughter of Duryodhana, from her svayamvara. The Kauravas capture him. Balarama goes to Hastinapura to negotiate but is insulted. Enraged, he uproots the city with his plow and is about to throw it into the Ganges. The Kauravas surrender and offer gifts, and Balarama forgives them.

story.title: The Abduction of Lakshmana and Balarama's Wrath
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the events of the Mahabharata war? Actually, this is before the war, as Duryodhana is alive and ruling.
story.comic_potential: High: Balarama uprooting the entire city of Hastinapura with his plow is a visually stunning and humorous scene, especially the Kauravas' panic.

teachings.teaching: Balarama's anger is for the sake of teaching a lesson, not out of hatred or envy.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that divine anger is a form of compassionate correction, not a personal reaction. It is a tool to bring about alignment with dharma.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the sole cause of creation, maintenance, and dissolution, and the universe is his plaything.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga understanding that the entire cosmos is a manifestation of the divine's playful energy (lila). The practitioner should see all events as part of this divine play.

teachings.teaching: Even the powerful should not be arrogant; pride leads to downfall.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes humility as a key virtue. The Kauravas' pride in their power and lineage blinds them to the divine presence in Balarama.

chapter_summary: Balarama returns to Dvaraka after his pilgrimage, recounts his adventures to the Yadava assembly, and the city still bears marks of his prowess.

story.title: Balarama's Return and Narration
story.arc: Balarama's pilgrimage and return
story.timeline_note: After the events of the pilgrimage, Balarama returns to Dvaraka.
story.comic_potential: Balarama recounting his exploits to the assembly, with dramatic gestures.

teachings.teaching: The city still shows signs of Balarama's past deeds, indicating that divine actions leave lasting impressions.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes that spiritual practices (kriyas) imprint on the practitioner's being and environment, manifesting as tangible results.

chapter_summary: Narada visits Dvaraka to see how Krishna simultaneously lives with his 16,108 wives. He witnesses Krishna in different forms in each palace, engaged in various activities, demonstrating his yoga-maya. Narada praises Krishna and departs, and the chapter concludes by glorifying those who hear or narrate this pastime.

story.title: Narada's Visit to Dvaraka: Krishna's Yoga-Maya Displayed
story.arc: Narada, curious about Krishna's marriage to 16,108 wives, goes to Dvaraka. He sees Krishna in each palace simultaneously performing different activities, realizing Krishna's divine yoga-maya. Narada praises Krishna and leaves, meditating on the Lord's pastimes.
story.timeline_note: After Narada's previous visit and after Krishna's marriage to many wives.
story.comic_potential: Narada's bewildered expressions as he moves from palace to palace, each time seeing Krishna in a different role, could be played for humor.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's simultaneous presence in multiple forms demonstrates his supreme yoga-maya power, which is beyond the comprehension of even great sages.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'vibhuti' or divine manifestation: the same consciousness can appear in multiple forms simultaneously, showing the non-dual nature of reality.

teachings.teaching: Narada's humility and devotion: he recognizes his limited understanding and seeks Krishna's grace to remember him.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that true knowledge comes from devotion and surrender, not intellectual effort alone.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's pastimes as a householder teach that one can live a worldly life while remaining spiritually detached.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that action in the world can be performed with inner detachment, as Krishna demonstrates by engaging in all activities while remaining the supreme controller.

chapter_summary: Krishna's daily routine in Dwaraka, his morning activities, audience in Sudharma assembly, arrival of a messenger from imprisoned kings, Narada's visit, and discussion about Yudhishthira's Rajasuya sacrifice.

story.title: Krishna's Daily Routine and the Imprisoned Kings
story.arc: Preparation for Rajasuya
story.timeline_note: Early morning to midday in Dwaraka, during the period of Yudhishthira's reign.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's playful departure from the inner palace, the messenger's dramatic plea, and Narada's sudden appearance.

teachings.teaching: The importance of daily spiritual practice: meditation, worship, and charity.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes disciplined daily sadhana as the foundation for inner transformation.

teachings.teaching: The illusory nature of material happiness and the need to surrender to Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as recognition of the ego's suffering and the call to align with the true Self.

teachings.teaching: The power of hearing, chanting, and meditating on Krishna's names and pastimes for purification.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga incorporates mantra meditation as a key practice for cleansing the mind.

chapter_summary: Uddhava advises Krishna to kill Jarāsandha to enable the Rājasūya sacrifice, and Krishna agrees. The Yadava army travels to Indraprastha, where Yudhiṣṭhira and the Pāṇḍavas welcome them. Krishna stays for several months, enjoying pastimes with Arjuna.

story.title: Uddhava's Counsel and Krishna's Journey to Indraprastha
story.arc: Preparation for Rājasūya
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's return from Dvārakā, before the Rājasūya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: The grand procession of the Yadava army and the emotional reunion with the Pāṇḍavas.

teachings.teaching: One should perform prescribed sacrifices like Rājasūya for the benefit of all.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes the importance of action (kriya) performed with detachment for the welfare of others, aligning with the principle of karma-yoga.

teachings.teaching: The Lord arranges circumstances to fulfill His devotees' desires and protect them.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework highlights that divine intervention is a form of grace (kripa) that supports the practitioner's efforts in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Devotees sing the glories of the Lord's pastimes, which purify the listener.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the practice of kirtan as a means to focus the mind and attain inner stillness, a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: Yudhiṣṭhira expresses his desire to perform the Rājasūya sacrifice, and Kṛṣṇa advises him to first conquer all kings. Yudhiṣṭhira sends his brothers in all directions; they subdue many kings and bring tribute. However, Jarāsandha remains unconquered. Kṛṣṇa, Bhīma, and Arjuna disguise themselves as brāhmaṇas, go to Girivraja, and challenge Jarāsandha to a duel. After a fierce battle, Bhīma kills Jarāsandha by splitting his body. Kṛṣṇa then releases the imprisoned kings and installs Jarāsandha's son Sahadeva as king of Magadha.

story.title: The Slaying of Jarāsandha
story.arc: Preparation for the Rājasūya sacrifice
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the Pāṇḍavas have established their kingdom and before the Rājasūya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: High: Disguised heroes, a boastful villain, a dramatic duel with a unique finishing move.

teachings.teaching: One should perform righteous acts like the Rājasūya sacrifice to attain fame and spiritual merit.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the principle of 'kriya' or action performed with detachment and dedication to the divine, leading to purification and liberation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial and bestows grace according to one's devotion, like a wish-fulfilling tree.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the divine responds to the sincere effort of the practitioner, not based on external status.

teachings.teaching: Even an unstable body can be used to attain eternal glory, as exemplified by kings like Hariścandra and Bali.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga teaching that the body is a temporary instrument for spiritual practice and should be used to achieve higher goals.

teachings.teaching: A true warrior should not hesitate to give even his life for the sake of brāhmaṇas or dharma.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the attitude of total surrender (ātma-nivedana) to the divine will.

chapter_summary: Krishna releases the kings imprisoned by Jarasandha, who praise him and renounce worldly desires. Krishna instructs them on devotion and dharma, then sends them back to their kingdoms after honoring them. He then has Bhima kill Jarasandha and returns to Indraprastha with Arjuna and Sahadeva, where Yudhishthira rejoices.

story.title: The Release of the Imprisoned Kings
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Mathura and Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Jarasandha's defeat and before the Rajasuya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic release of the kings, their emotional prostration, and Krishna's majestic appearance.

teachings.teaching: Wealth and power lead to pride and downfall; one should seek refuge in Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this echoes the need to transcend ego and material attachments through disciplined practice and surrender to the divine.

teachings.teaching: The world is like a mirage; only the Supreme Reality is eternal.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes discerning the transient from the eternal, aligning with this teaching of māyā.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Krishna leads to liberation from the cycle of birth and death.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga goal of realizing the Self through devotion and meditation.

teachings.teaching: One should perform duties without attachment, keeping mind fixed on Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga teaches detached action (karma-yoga) as a means to purify the mind and attain union with the divine.

chapter_summary: Yudhishthira praises Krishna after hearing of Jarāsandha's defeat, then performs the Rājasūya sacrifice with many sages and kings. During the ceremony, Sahadeva proposes offering the first honor to Krishna, which enrages Śiśupāla. Śiśupāla insults Krishna and the assembly, leading to Krishna beheading him with his discus. Śiśupāla's soul merges into Krishna. The sacrifice concludes, and Krishna returns to Dvārakā.

story.title: The Rājasūya Sacrifice and the Death of Śiśupāla
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes as the supreme lord
story.timeline_note: After Jarāsandha's death, Yudhishthira performs Rājasūya. Śiśupāla's death occurs during the ceremony.
story.comic_potential: High drama: Sahadeva's bold proposal, Śiśupāla's angry outburst, Krishna's calm beheading, and the soul merging like a meteor.

teachings.teaching: Krishna is the supreme, non-dual reality, the source of all deities, sacrifices, and spiritual paths.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this affirms that the ultimate goal of all practices is to realize the oneness of the self with Krishna, the inner controller.

teachings.teaching: Offering respect to Krishna is equivalent to offering respect to all beings and oneself.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga principle of seeing the divine in all, leading to universal love and service.

teachings.teaching: Constant enmity and meditation on an enemy can lead to merging with that entity, as Śiśupāla's hatred for Krishna led to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes the power of focused attention; even negative focus can lead to union, but devotion is the direct path.

teachings.teaching: One who hears blasphemy of the Lord and does not leave is fallen.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the importance of association with devotees and avoiding offenses, a key practice in Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: The chapter describes the conclusion of the Rajasuya sacrifice, where all beings praise Krishna and the sacrifice, and then return to their abodes except the sinful Duryodhana, who cannot bear the prosperity of the Pandavas. It concludes with a verse glorifying the recitation of Krishna's deeds, including the killing of Shishupala and the liberation of the king.

story.title: Conclusion of Rajasuya and Duryodhana's Envy
story.arc: Rajasuya Sacrifice
story.timeline_note: After the Rajasuya sacrifice, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Duryodhana's envy could be depicted with a dark cloud over him while others rejoice.

teachings.teaching: Recitation of Krishna's deeds (like killing Shishupala) liberates from all sins.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the power of kirtana (chanting) as a purifying practice that aligns the practitioner with divine will.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the grand Rājasūya sacrifice of King Yudhiṣṭhira, the distribution of duties among his brothers and relatives, the joyous celebrations including music, dance, and the avabhṛtha bath in the Gaṅgā. It then narrates Duryodhana's envy upon seeing the opulence, his humiliation due to Māyā's illusion in the assembly hall, and his angry departure, which foreshadows future conflict.

story.title: The Rājasūya of Yudhiṣṭhira and Duryodhana's Humiliation
story.arc: This chapter depicts the successful completion of Yudhiṣṭhira's Rājasūya sacrifice, the joyous celebrations, and the subsequent envy and humiliation of Duryodhana, which sows the seeds for the great war.
story.timeline_note: This event occurs after the Pāṇḍavas have established their kingdom and before the exile and the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: The scene of Duryodhana mistaking water for land and land for water due to Māyā's illusion, and then being laughed at by Bhīma and others, has strong comic potential.

teachings.teaching: The Rājasūya sacrifice demonstrates the ideal of righteous kingship and the importance of performing one's duties with devotion.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of karma-yoga: performing actions as an offering to the divine, without attachment to results.

teachings.teaching: The avabhṛtha bath symbolizes purification from sins and the renewal of spiritual life.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the cleansing of the subtle body through disciplined practice, leading to inner purity.

teachings.teaching: Duryodhana's envy and humiliation illustrate the dangers of pride and attachment to material opulence.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on detachment (vairāgya) and the need to overcome the ego.

teachings.teaching: The illusion (Māyā) that confuses Duryodhana shows how ignorance leads to suffering.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a metaphor for the veiling power of prakṛti, which can only be transcended through self-realization and divine grace.

chapter_summary: Shalva, a friend of Shishupala, vows to destroy the Yadavas after being defeated at Rukmini's wedding. He propitiates Lord Shiva and receives a flying city called Saubha, built by Maya Danava. Shalva attacks Dvaraka with Saubha, causing devastation. Pradyumna leads the Yadava defense, countering Shalva's illusions with divine weapons. During the battle, Pradyumna is struck by Shalva's minister Dyuman and is carried away by his charioteer, but regains consciousness and rebukes the charioteer for retreating. The charioteer explains he acted out of duty to protect Pradyumna.

story.title: The Slaying of Shalva
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Rukmini's wedding, before Krishna's later pastimes.
story.comic_potential: High; flying city Saubha, illusions, divine weapons, and dramatic chariot retreat.

teachings.teaching: Even a powerful king like Shalva, deluded by pride, makes a vow to destroy the Yadavas, but ultimately fails due to divine protection.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates how ego-driven actions (kama) lead to bondage, while surrender to the divine (Ishvara-pranidhana) brings protection.

teachings.teaching: Shalva's austerity to Shiva shows that even demons can attain boons through tapas, but misuse leads to destruction.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that tapas (austerity) must be coupled with sattvic intent; otherwise, it strengthens the ego and leads to downfall.

teachings.teaching: Pradyumna's rebuke of his charioteer for retreating highlights the kshatriya duty to never flee from battle.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of dharma (right action) without attachment to outcomes; Pradyumna's anger stems from ego, not duty.

teachings.teaching: The charioteer's response that protecting a fallen warrior is dharma shows the importance of discernment in duty.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would note that this illustrates viveka (discernment) in applying dharma according to circumstances, a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: Krishna battles Shalva, who uses illusion to fake the death of Vasudeva. Krishna overcomes the illusion, kills Shalva, and destroys the Saubha flying city. Dantavakra then arrives seeking revenge.

story.title: The Slaying of Shalva and the Destruction of Saubha
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes as the Supreme Lord, demonstrating his power over illusion and his protection of devotees.
story.timeline_note: After the Rajasuya sacrifice and Shishupala's death, Krishna returns to Dvaraka and fights Shalva.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic battle scenes, illusion of Vasudeva's death, flying city Saubha, Krishna's anger and subsequent calm, beheading of Shalva.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's apparent human emotions (grief, anger) are part of his lila and do not affect his supreme knowledge.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that the realized yogi can act in the world with full engagement while remaining internally detached, knowing the Self is untouched.

teachings.teaching: Illusions (maya) are overcome by the Lord's power; devotees should take shelter of him.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that through Kriya-Yoga, one can pierce the veil of maya and realize the true self, just as Krishna saw through Shalva's illusion.

teachings.teaching: True heroes act rather than boast; action is superior to empty words.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on practice (kriya) over mere intellectual understanding.

chapter_summary: Krishna kills Dantavakra and his brother Viduratha, then returns to Dvaraka. Balarama, avoiding the Kuru-Pandava war, goes on a pilgrimage. At Naimisha forest, he kills the arrogant suta Romaharshana for disrespecting sages, then accepts penance to travel across Bharatavarsha for twelve months.

entities.name: Balvala
aliases: ilvalasya suto ghoro balvalo nāma dānavaḥ

story.title: Krishna kills Dantavakra and Balarama's pilgrimage and slaying of Romaharshana
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes and Balarama's pilgrimage
