teachings.lens_note: Sharma warns that attachment to siddhis leads to spiritual pride and deviation from the goal of self-realization; they should be seen as byproducts, not goals.

teachings.teaching: Krishna is the sole lord and source of all siddhis, yoga, sāṅkhya, dharma, and the knowledge of the Vedas.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the ultimate non-dual truth: all powers and paths originate from and culminate in the divine consciousness.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna describes his various vibhutis (divine manifestations) to Uddhava, explaining that he is the essence and supreme being in all categories of existence. He concludes by instructing Uddhava to control speech, mind, and senses through devotion.

story.title: The Vibhuti Yoga: Krishna's Divine Manifestations
story.arc: Uddhava asks Krishna to explain how the wise worship him in various forms; Krishna responds by enumerating his vibhutis and concludes with instructions on controlling the mind and senses.
story.timeline_note: After the Mahabharata war, Krishna instructs Uddhava before his departure.
story.comic_potential: A cosmic catalog of divine identities, each verse a cameo of a deity or sage, culminating in a meditative practice.

teachings.teaching: Krishna is the supreme being, the source and essence of all existence.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this affirms that the ultimate reality is both immanent and transcendent, accessible through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: All divine manifestations are aspects of Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: This supports the Kriya-Yoga view that all deities are expressions of one consciousness, aiding meditation on unity.

teachings.teaching: Control of speech, mind, and senses is essential for spiritual progress.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes pratyahara (sense withdrawal) and dharana (concentration) as foundational Kriya-Yoga practices.

chapter_summary: Uddhava asks Krishna to explain the eternal dharma characterized by devotion, which was previously taught to Brahma in the form of a swan. Krishna describes the original varṇa system in Kṛta-yuga, the emergence of the four varṇas and āśramas from his body, their inherent qualities, and the duties of a brahmacārī, including respect for the guru and celibacy. He also discusses the duties of a brāhmaṇa, the king's role in protection, and how to overcome adversity without resorting to degrading occupations.

story.title: The Eternal Dharma of Varṇāśrama and Devotion
story.arc: Uddhava inquires about the dharma characterized by devotion; Krishna explains the origin and duties of varṇas and āśramas.
story.timeline_note: Set after Krishna's discourse on bhakti-yoga; references Kṛta-yuga and Tretā-yuga.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's description of the guru as 'all gods' and the strict rules for brahmacārīs could be depicted humorously.

teachings.teaching: The original varṇa in Kṛta-yuga was 'haṃsa' (swan), and people were naturally perfect.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the state of innate purity and effortless alignment with dharma.

teachings.teaching: The four varṇas (brāhmaṇa, kṣatriya, vaiśya, śūdra) and four āśramas (brahmacarya, gṛhastha, vānaprastha, sannyāsa) originate from different parts of the cosmic body.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a map of the human constitution: head (brāhmaṇa), arms (kṣatriya), thighs (vaiśya), feet (śūdra) — corresponding to cognitive, protective, productive, and service functions.

teachings.teaching: The natural qualities of each varṇa: brāhmaṇa — śama, dama, tapaḥ, śauca, santoṣa, kṣānti, ārjavam, bhakti, dayā, satyam; kṣatriya — tejo, balaṃ, dhṛtiḥ, śauryaṃ, titikṣā, audāryaṃ, udyamaḥ, sthairyaṃ, brahmanyam, aiśvaryaṃ; vaiśya — āstikyaṃ, dāna-niṣṭhā, adambho, brahma-sevanam, atuṣṭir arthopacayaiḥ; śūdra — śuśrūṣaṇaṃ, santoṣaḥ.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes cultivating these qualities through practice; they are not mere labels but active disciplines.

teachings.teaching: The universal dharma for all varṇas: ahiṃsā, satyam, asteyam, akāma-krodha-lobhatā, bhūta-priya-hitehā.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would highlight these as foundational ethical practices that prepare the mind for deeper Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: A brahmacārī should serve the guru with humility, celibacy, and strict discipline, seeing the guru as non-different from Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's framework, guru-sevā is a form of Kriya-Yoga, purifying the mind and channeling energy upward.

teachings.teaching: After completing brahmacarya, one may enter gṛhastha, vānaprastha, or sannyāsa, but should not remain without an āśrama.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would stress the importance of structured life stages for spiritual progress; each stage has its own discipline.

teachings.teaching: A brāhmaṇa should avoid accepting gifts (pratigraha) as it diminishes austerity; he may live by ṛta (gleaning) or amṛta (alms).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a call to minimize material dependencies to focus on inner practice.

teachings.teaching: A king should protect his subjects like a father, and if a brāhmaṇa is in distress, the king should rescue him.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as the kṣatriya's dharma of selfless service, a form of karma-yoga.

teachings.teaching: In times of adversity, one may adopt a lower varṇa's occupation but never a dog's occupation (śva-vṛtti).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would emphasize maintaining dignity and ethical standards even in hardship.

teachings.teaching: One should perform daily sacrifices (deva, ṛṣi, pitṛ, bhūta) and not be attached to family.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see these as practices that integrate the individual with cosmic rhythms, reducing ego.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (11.17.52-58) discusses detachment from family life, emphasizing that relationships are temporary like a traveler's meeting, and that one should not be attached to home and family. It warns against the bondage caused by attachment to wife, children, and wealth, and advises living as a guest in one's own home, free from possessiveness and ego. The chapter contrasts the wise person who sees the impermanent nature of worldly ties with the fool who is obsessed with family and falls into darkness after death.

story.title: Detachment from Family Life
story.arc: Instruction on renunciation and the dangers of attachment
story.timeline_note: Part of Krishna's teachings to Uddhava in the Bhagavata Purana, likely after the Mahabharata war.
story.comic_potential: A dramatic contrast between the wise person calmly living as a guest and the frantic fool worrying about family, with vivid imagery of darkness after death.

teachings.teaching: Do not be attached to family members; do not be negligent even as a householder. The wise see the impermanent as impermanent, even if unseen.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the practice of detachment (vairagya) as essential for inner stillness and union with the divine. The 'unseen' refers to the subtle reality beyond sensory perception.

teachings.teaching: Association with sons, wife, and relatives is like the meeting of travelers; they separate after each body, like a dream that ends upon waking.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a reminder that all relationships are temporary roles in the drama of life; the true self is the witness, not the actor.

teachings.teaching: Reflecting thus, one should live free in houses like a guest, not bound by homes, without possessiveness or ego.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga ideal of 'sthita-prajna' (steady wisdom) where one performs duties without attachment, seeing the body and home as temporary shelters.

teachings.teaching: A devotee may worship Me through householder duties, then retire to the forest or renounce; but one attached to home, obsessed with children and wealth, is bound by 'I' and 'mine'.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would stress that the path of action (karma-yoga) must be done with devotion and detachment; otherwise, it leads to bondage. The 'forest' symbolizes inner solitude.

teachings.teaching: The fool thinks, 'Oh, my aged parents, wife, young children are helpless without me; how will they live in misery?' Thus his heart is pierced by household anxiety, and after death he enters blinding darkness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would identify this as the result of ignorance (avidya) and attachment (raga), which create karmic bonds. The 'blinding darkness' is the state of unconsciousness after death, from which liberation is sought through Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna describes the duties and lifestyle of the vānaprastha (forest-dweller) and sannyāsa (renunciate) āśramas, emphasizing detachment, self-control, and devotion to Him as the ultimate goal. He outlines practices such as living on forest foods, performing austerities, and eventually renouncing all actions to attain liberation.

story.title: The Duties of the Vānaprastha and Sannyāsī
story.arc: Krishna instructs Uddhava on the progressive stages of renunciation and the path to liberation through devotion.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Uddhava Gītā discourse before Krishna's departure.
story.comic_potential: A serene forest scene with Krishna explaining ascetic practices to Uddhava, interspersed with symbolic imagery of the moon reflecting in water.

teachings.teaching: The vānaprastha should live in the forest, subsist on wild roots and fruits, wear bark or deerskin, and perform austerities like the five fires in summer and standing in water in winter.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, these external austerities symbolize internal purification and control of the senses, preparing the mind for deeper meditation.

teachings.teaching: The sannyāsī should renounce all possessions, beg for food, and wander alone, controlling the mind and senses, seeing the self as non-different from Brahman.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that true renunciation is internal; the sannyāsī's external wandering mirrors the inner journey of detaching from ego and realizing unity with the divine.

teachings.teaching: One should not be attached to any sect or doctrine, but remain equipoised, tolerating insults, and seeing the same self in all beings.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's teaching of 'sama-darśana' (equal vision) as a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga, where the practitioner sees the divine presence in all.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Krishna, performed according to one's dharma, leads to firm bhakti and ultimately to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga integrates action (karma) and devotion (bhakti) as complementary paths; performing one's duties with devotion purifies the heart.

chapter_summary: In this chapter, Lord Krishna defines the nature of true knowledge, devotion, and the path to liberation. He explains that jñāna (knowledge) is seeing the one reality behind all beings, and vijñāna (realization) is perceiving the threefold transformations of matter. He then describes bhakti-yoga as the supreme means to attain Him, listing various practices such as hearing, chanting, worship, and service. Uddhava asks for definitions of many spiritual terms (yama, niyama, śama, dama, etc.), and Krishna answers each, culminating in the teaching that the highest good is to transcend both good and bad qualities.

story.title: The Definition of True Knowledge and Devotion
story.arc: Uddhava asks Krishna to explain the essence of spiritual practice; Krishna responds by defining jñāna, vijñāna, bhakti, and various virtues.
story.timeline_note: After the Mahābhārata war, Yudhiṣṭhira asked Bhīṣma about dharma; now Krishna recounts that teaching to Uddhava.
story.comic_potential: A dialogue-heavy exposition with a series of definitions, suitable for an illustrated glossary or a teaching panel.

teachings.teaching: True knowledge (jñāna) is seeing the one reality in all beings, and vijñāna is perceiving the threefold transformations of matter.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the unity of consciousness (jñāna) and the practical discernment of the three guṇas (vijñāna) in one's own experience.

teachings.teaching: Bhakti-yoga consists of hearing, chanting, worship, service, and offering all actions to the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that these practices are the external expressions of an internal attitude of surrender, which is the essence of Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: The highest dharma is devotion to the Lord; knowledge is seeing the oneness of the self; detachment is non-attachment to the guṇas; and aiśvarya is the eight siddhis.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets aiśvarya not as supernatural powers but as the natural mastery that comes from self-realization.

teachings.teaching: The definitions of yama and niyama include twelve each: ahiṃsā, satya, asteya, asaṅga, hrī, asañcaya, āstikya, brahmacarya, mauna, sthairya, kṣamā, abhayam; and śauca, japa, tapas, homa, śraddhā, atithya, mad-arcana, tīrthāṭana, parārthehā, tuṣṭi, ācārya-sevanam.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga incorporates these as foundational ethical disciplines for the practitioner.

teachings.teaching: The ultimate good is to transcend both good and bad qualities; the wise person is free from the duality of guṇa and doṣa.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's teaching of going beyond the pairs of opposites through Kriya-Yoga meditation.

chapter_summary: Krishna explains the three paths of yoga (jñāna, karma, bhakti) suited to different temperaments, emphasizing bhakti-yoga as the supreme means to liberation. He describes the process of detachment, mind control, and the ultimate goal of exclusive devotion, which surpasses even liberation.

story.title: The Three Paths of Yoga and the Supremacy of Bhakti
story.arc: Uddhava questions the apparent contradictions in Vedic injunctions; Krishna responds by delineating the three yogas and extolling bhakti.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Uddhava Gītā discourse before Krishna's departure.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's vivid analogies (bird leaving nest, boat crossing ocean) could be illustrated.

teachings.teaching: The three yogas (jñāna, karma, bhakti) are prescribed according to one's stage of detachment and faith.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: The three yogas correspond to stages of inner purification; bhakti-yoga integrates action and knowledge through devotion.

teachings.teaching: One should perform prescribed duties without desire until detachment arises, then take up bhakti.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga: Karma-yoga purifies the mind, preparing it for bhakti, which is the direct means to realize the Self.

teachings.teaching: The human body is a rare boat to cross the ocean of material existence; one who does not use it is a self-destroyer.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga: The body is the instrument for kriya (action) leading to liberation; neglecting it is spiritual suicide.

teachings.teaching: Mind control is achieved through steady practice, detachment, and fixing the mind on the Self or on Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga: Mind control is the essence of yoga; techniques like pranayama and meditation are means to still the mind.

teachings.teaching: Bhakti-yoga destroys all karmas, doubts, and knots of the heart; it surpasses jñāna and vairāgya.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga: Bhakti is the culmination of Kriya-Yoga, where devotion transforms every action into an offering.

teachings.teaching: Pure devotees do not desire even liberation; they seek only exclusive devotion to Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga: The highest stage is nirapekṣa (desireless) bhakti, where the devotee acts without any personal motive.

chapter_summary: The Lord explains the nature of dharma and adharma, the role of the Vedas, the process of purification, and the dangers of sense attachment. He emphasizes that the ultimate purpose of all scriptures is to lead to Him, and that mere ritualistic or fruitive activities without devotion are futile.

story.title: The Lord's Discourse on Dharma and the Vedas
story.arc: Teaching
story.timeline_note: Part of the Uddhava Gita, after Kṛṣṇa's departure from Mathura.
story.comic_potential: A philosophical dialogue with minimal action; could be illustrated as a serene teaching scene.

teachings.teaching: One's dedication to one's prescribed duty is a virtue; deviation is a fault.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes steadfastness in one's chosen spiritual practice (sadhana) as the foundation for progress.

teachings.teaching: Purity and impurity are relative; they are determined by context and purpose.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees this as a call to discern the inner purity of intention over external ritualistic cleanliness.

teachings.teaching: The Vedas prescribe duties according to time, place, and person to regulate actions.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on adapting practice to one's capacity and circumstances.

teachings.teaching: Purification is achieved through various means including water, time, and remembrance of Me.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, remembrance of the Lord (smaraṇa) is the ultimate purifier, surpassing external methods.

teachings.teaching: Dharma is perfected by six factors: knowledge of mantras, purity of action, and offering to Me.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga stresses that all actions should be offered to the divine, transforming them into yoga.

teachings.teaching: Attachment to sense objects leads to desire, anger, delusion, and loss of self-awareness.
teachings.lens_note: This is the core of Kriya-Yoga's teaching: sense control is essential to still the mind and realize the Self.

teachings.teaching: The promise of heavenly rewards in the Vedas is meant to attract the ignorant, not the wise.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views such promises as provisional; the true goal is liberation, not temporary pleasures.

teachings.teaching: Those who are attached to rituals and rewards do not know Me, who dwells in the heart.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga emphasizes direct inner realization over external ritualism.

teachings.teaching: The Vedas ultimately describe Me; all their words point to Me and lead to Me.
teachings.lens_note: This is the essence of Kriya-Yoga: the scriptures are a means to realize the indwelling Lord.

chapter_summary: Uddhava asks Krishna about the number of tattvas (elements) enumerated by various sages. Krishna explains that the differences arise from the interplay of his maya, and that all enumerations are valid from their respective perspectives. He describes the evolution of prakriti and purusha, the three gunas, the senses, and the process of transmigration. The chapter emphasizes that disputes about categories are due to ignorance of the self, and that the true nature is realized through self-knowledge.

story.title: The Enumeration of Tattvas
story.arc: Uddhava's questions on Sankhya and Vedanta
story.timeline_note: Part of the Uddhava Gita discourse before Krishna's departure
story.comic_potential: A philosophical dialogue with no dramatic action; could be visualized as a teaching session.

teachings.teaching: The different enumerations of tattvas by sages are all valid from their own perspectives, arising from the interplay of maya.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that intellectual disputes about categories are secondary; direct experience of the self through practice is primary.

teachings.teaching: The self (atman) cannot be known by itself due to beginningless ignorance; a teacher who knows the truth is needed.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga stresses the necessity of a guru's grace and the practice of yoga to overcome ignorance.

teachings.teaching: Prakriti and purusha are distinct, but due to their mutual interpenetration, they appear non-different.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this points to the need to discriminate between the seer and the seen through meditative practice.

teachings.teaching: The mind, driven by karma, leads the soul from one body to another; attachment to sense objects causes forgetfulness of the self.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that through pranayama and meditation, one can still the mind and remember the self.

teachings.teaching: The states of life (birth, childhood, etc.) are like a dream; the self is unchanging.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga practice helps one realize the unchanging witness beyond the changing states.

chapter_summary: In Bhagavata Purana 11.22, Krishna explains to Uddhava the nature of the self and the world, emphasizing that the soul is distinct from the body and the material qualities (sattva, rajas, tamas). He describes how the unwise person, due to ignorance, becomes entangled in samsara, while the wise person sees the self as separate. The chapter uses analogies like a tree and its seed, a dancer and spectator, and moving water and trees to illustrate the relationship between the self and the material world. Uddhava then asks how one can overcome the powerful influence of material nature.

story.title: Krishna's Discourse on the Self and Material Nature
story.arc: Uddhava's questions and Krishna's answers on the nature of reality and liberation
story.timeline_note: Part of the larger Uddhava Gita section in Bhagavata Purana, after the departure of the Yadus.
story.comic_potential: A philosophical dialogue with analogies that could be visually depicted, such as the tree and seed, dancer and spectator.

teachings.teaching: The self (atman) is distinct from the body and material nature, just as a seer of a tree is different from the tree and its seed and fruit.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the need to disidentify from the body and mind through meditative practices, realizing the self as a witness.

teachings.teaching: The unwise person, not discriminating the self from material nature, becomes deluded by sense contact and enters samsara, taking births among gods, humans, or animals according to the modes of nature.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would highlight that ignorance of one's true nature leads to karmic bondage; Kriya-Yoga aims to break this cycle through self-awareness.

teachings.teaching: Just as one imitates a dancer or singer by watching, the self, though inactive, appears to act due to the qualities of the intellect (buddhi).
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the concept of 'witnessing' in Kriya-Yoga, where the practitioner observes mental modifications without identification.

teachings.teaching: Analogies of moving water making trees seem to move, and a spinning eye making the earth seem to spin, show that the self's perception of change is due to the mind's activity.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would use these to explain that the world's apparent reality is a projection of the mind; Kriya-Yoga stills the mind to perceive the unchanging self.

teachings.teaching: The experiences of the mind (like daydreams and dreams) are false; similarly, the cycle of samsara is unreal for the self.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga view that the world is a temporary manifestation; liberation comes from realizing its illusory nature.

teachings.teaching: Even though the objects are unreal, samsara continues due to thinking about sense objects, like experiencing misfortune in a dream.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that mental attachment perpetuates bondage; Kriya-Yoga practices detachment and inner focus.

teachings.teaching: Therefore, Uddhava should not enjoy sense objects with the false senses, but see the world as a mental construct and a mistake.
teachings.lens_note: This is a direct instruction for renunciation and discrimination, core to Kriya-Yoga's path of inner purification.

teachings.teaching: One who is insulted, abused, or harmed by ignorant people should, desiring the highest good, lift oneself by oneself when in difficulty.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as self-reliance through spiritual practice; even in adversity, one must use discrimination and meditation to rise above.

teachings.teaching: Uddhava asks how one can understand this teaching, as material nature is very powerful even for the wise, except for those devoted to Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would note that devotion (bhakti) combined with Kriya-Yoga is the ultimate shelter to overcome material nature.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna narrates to Uddhava the story of a greedy brahmana from Avanti who lost his wealth and family, became a mendicant, and endured insults from wicked people. Through this, he teaches that the mind is the root cause of bondage and liberation, and that controlling the mind is the essence of all spiritual practices.

story.title: The Story of the Avanti Brahmana
story.arc: Krishna tells Uddhava the story of a wealthy but miserly brahmana who lost everything due to his own faults, became a wandering mendicant, and was insulted by wicked people. He realized the mind is the cause of bondage and attained peace.
story.timeline_note: Narrated by Krishna to Uddhava as a past event.
story.comic_potential: The brahmana's fall from wealth to poverty and his endurance of insults could be depicted humorously, highlighting the irony of his earlier greed.

teachings.teaching: The mind is the root cause of all suffering and bondage; controlling the mind is the highest yoga.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the practice of pratyahara and dharana to still the mind and realize the self.

teachings.teaching: Wealth and material pursuits lead to misery; one should detach from them and seek spiritual goals.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that kriya-yoga purifies the mind, enabling detachment from artha and kama.

teachings.teaching: The wise person endures insults and hardships, understanding they are results of past karma.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the kriya-yoga principle of accepting prarabdha karma while practicing inner purification.

teachings.teaching: Human birth is rare and should be used for spiritual liberation, not for accumulating wealth.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework stresses the urgency of using this human body for sadhana.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (11.23.50-60) presents a philosophical discourse on the causes of suffering and duality. It systematically refutes external causes (deities, body, planets, karma, time) and identifies the true cause as the mind's delusion (ātma-vibhrama). The solution is to control the mind through devotion to Mukunda (Krishna), as taught by a sage who remained unshaken despite adversity.

story.title: The Sage's Song on Renunciation
story.arc: Philosophical discourse on the causes of suffering and the path to liberation
story.timeline_note: Part of the Bhagavata Purana's eleventh canto, after the destruction of the Yadu dynasty.
story.comic_potential: A sage, having lost all possessions, wanders the earth and is mistreated by lowly people, yet remains unshaken, teaching a profound lesson on the nature of duality.

teachings.teaching: Suffering is not caused by external factors (deities, body, planets, karma, time) but by the mind's delusion (ātma-vibhrama).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the principle that the mind's identification with the body and external events creates suffering; through inner discipline, one realizes the self as distinct from these.

teachings.teaching: The enlightened person, established in the self, does not fear any being.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes fearlessness as a result of realizing one's true nature beyond the dualities of pleasure and pain.

teachings.teaching: One should cross the ocean of darkness by exclusive devotion to Mukunda's lotus feet.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, devotion (bhakti) is the culmination of practice, where the mind is fixed on the divine, leading to liberation.

teachings.teaching: The sage, though mistreated, remained unshaken in his duty, teaching that one should not be disturbed by external circumstances.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches equanimity in all situations as a sign of spiritual maturity.

teachings.teaching: The mind must be controlled with intelligence fixed on the Lord; this is the essence of yoga.
teachings.lens_note: This directly echoes Kriya-Yoga's core practice of controlling the mind through meditation and devotion.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna explains the Sāṅkhya philosophy of creation and dissolution, describing how the one undivided reality manifests as Prakṛti and Puruṣa, evolves into the guṇas, mahat, ahaṅkāra, tan-mātras, indriyas, devatās, and the cosmic egg, and ultimately dissolves back into the supreme self. He emphasizes that understanding this process destroys doubt and illusion.

story.title: Sāṅkhya: The Path of Analytical Knowledge
story.arc: Lord Krishna teaches Uddhava the Sāṅkhya philosophy, describing the evolution and involution of the cosmos, culminating in the realization of the supreme self.
story.timeline_note: This teaching occurs in the context of Krishna's final instructions to Uddhava before his departure.
story.comic_potential: The abstract philosophical discourse is not inherently comic, but the vivid imagery of cosmic creation and dissolution could be visually striking.

teachings.teaching: The one undivided reality appears as the duality of Prakṛti and Puruṣa.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the initial separation of consciousness (Puruṣa) from its dynamic energy (Prakṛti) in meditation, where the yogi discerns the witness from the witnessed.

teachings.teaching: From Prakṛti, the three guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) arise, and from them evolves the entire cosmic hierarchy.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the guṇas are the fundamental forces that the yogi must transcend through Kriya-Yoga, which balances and sublimates them.

teachings.teaching: The ego (ahaṅkāra) is the root of illusion and bondage.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that Kriya-Yoga dissolves the ego by uniting individual consciousness with cosmic consciousness, thereby ending the cycle of rebirth.

teachings.teaching: The entire creation is a projection of the Lord, who is both the material and efficient cause.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's view that the universe is a manifestation of divine consciousness, and Kriya-Yoga enables direct perception of this unity.

teachings.teaching: Dissolution follows the reverse order of creation, ultimately merging into the supreme self.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga involves a similar involution process in meditation, where the yogi withdraws the senses and mind into the self, experiencing the dissolution of the universe within.

teachings.teaching: Knowledge of this Sāṅkhya cuts the knot of doubt and leads to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma asserts that intellectual understanding combined with Kriya-Yoga practice destroys all mental conditioning and reveals the self as pure consciousness.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna explains to Uddhava the three modes of material nature (sattva, rajas, tamas) and their manifestations in human qualities, actions, knowledge, faith, and lifestyle. He describes how to transcend the modes through bhakti-yoga and attain liberation.

story.title: The Modes of Nature and Transcendence
story.arc: Krishna instructs Uddhava on the three guṇas and the path to transcend them through devotion.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Uddhava Gita discourse before Krishna's departure.
story.comic_potential: None

teachings.teaching: The three modes (sattva, rajas, tamas) determine one's qualities and actions. Sattva brings happiness and knowledge, rajas leads to desire and activity, tamas causes ignorance and inertia.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the purification of the subtle body through disciplined practice, where sattva is cultivated to transcend the lower modes.

teachings.teaching: When one worships the Lord with devotion and without expectation, they are considered to be in sattva. Worship with desires is rajasic, and with violence is tamasic.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes nirapekṣa (detachment) as key to Kriya-Yoga, aligning with the sattvic worship described here.

teachings.teaching: The modes bind the soul; by conquering them through bhakti-yoga, one attains liberation and realizes oneness with Brahman.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga aims at direct realization of the Self through meditation, which is the practical application of this teaching.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna teaches Uddhava about detachment from sense objects, especially women, using the story of King Pururava (Aila) who became infatuated with the celestial nymph Urvashi. After losing her, he realized the futility of lust and attachment, and through self-realization, attained liberation. Krishna emphasizes avoiding bad association and seeking the company of saints, which leads to devotion and ultimate freedom.

story.title: The Story of King Pururava and Urvashi
story.arc: King Pururava becomes obsessed with Urvashi, loses her, laments, and eventually gains detachment and liberation through self-realization.
story.timeline_note: Past event narrated by Krishna to Uddhava.
story.comic_potential: The king's dramatic lamentation and realization of his foolishness could be portrayed with exaggerated emotions.

teachings.teaching: One should not associate with those who are lusty and attached to the body; such association leads to downfall.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this warns against sensory distractions that disrupt inner stillness and meditation.

teachings.teaching: The body is impure and temporary; attachment to it and to women is based on ignorance.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes discerning the true self from the physical body, a key step in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Association with saints (sadhus) cuts the knots of attachment and leads to devotion.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, satsang is crucial for purifying the mind and advancing in practice.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Krishna is the ultimate goal; once attained, nothing else remains.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma sees bhakti as the culmination of Kriya-Yoga, uniting the practitioner with the divine.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna explains the detailed procedure of kriyā-yoga, the method of worship through action, covering both Vedic and Tantric modes. He describes the preparation, types of images, offerings, and the ultimate goal of attaining liberation or divine association.

story.title: Kriyā-Yoga: The Path of Worship
story.arc: Uddhava requests Lord Krishna to explain the method of worship (kriyā-yoga) for attaining liberation. Krishna responds by detailing the procedures for arcā (image worship), fire sacrifices, and the benefits of establishing temples and gardens.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Uddhava Gītā, after the departure of the Yadus.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily instructional with no dramatic events.

teachings.teaching: Kriyā-yoga is the method of worshiping the Lord through prescribed actions, combining Vedic and Tantric rituals.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that action itself, when done with devotion and proper technique, becomes a direct means of spiritual realization, not merely a preliminary step.

teachings.teaching: The Lord can be worshiped in various forms: arcā (image), sthaṇḍila (ground), agni (fire), sūrya (sun), water, or in the heart.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees these as progressive stages of internalization, culminating in heart-centered worship where the practitioner realizes the Lord as the inner Self.

teachings.teaching: The eight types of images (śailī, dāru-mayī, etc.) accommodate different capacities and resources.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the principle of adaptability in Kriya-Yoga: the method must suit the practitioner's level, yet all lead to the same goal when performed with faith.

teachings.teaching: Worship with devotion, even if only water is offered, pleases the Lord more than abundant offerings from a non-devotee.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the inner attitude (bhāva) is the essence; Kriya-Yoga is not mere external ritual but a heart-centered practice.

teachings.teaching: The ultimate goal of kriyā-yoga is to attain siddhi (perfection) and even sārṣṭitā (equal opulence) with the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the culmination of Kriya-Yoga: the practitioner, through disciplined action, becomes a co-creator with the Divine, realizing their own divine nature.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana discusses the results of worship, including attaining Brahma-loka or equality with the Lord through three types of worship, and emphasizes that only through detached bhakti-yoga one attains the Lord. It also warns against stealing the livelihood of brahmins and describes the karmic consequences for various participants in actions.

story.title: Results of Worship and Karma
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: Timeless teaching
story.comic_potential: Low

teachings.teaching: Through worship with offerings, one attains Brahma-loka; through three types of worship, one attains equality with the Lord; but only through detached bhakti-yoga one attains the Lord directly.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that external rituals (puja) yield limited results, while inner, detached devotion (bhakti-yoga) leads to union with the divine.

teachings.teaching: One who steals the livelihood of a brahmin or a god becomes an eater of feces for millions of years.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a karmic consequence of violating dharma, highlighting the importance of righteous livelihood.

teachings.teaching: The doer, the charioteer, the instigator, and the approver all share in the karma and its results proportionally.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's emphasis on collective responsibility and the subtle effects of intention in action.

chapter_summary: In this chapter, Lord Krishna instructs Uddhava on the nature of the self and the world, emphasizing non-attachment to dualities, the illusory nature of material existence, and the path to liberation through knowledge, discrimination, and devotion. Uddhava questions the cause of samsara, and Krishna explains that it arises from identification with the body and mind, which can be overcome by realizing the self as pure consciousness. The chapter also discusses obstacles in yoga and methods to overcome them, including devotion, mantra, and meditation.

story.title: The Yoga of Discrimination and Devotion
story.arc: Uddhava questions the nature of samsara and the self; Krishna responds with a discourse on non-duality, the illusory nature of the world, and the means to attain liberation through knowledge and devotion.
story.timeline_note: This is part of the Bhagavata Purana, occurring after the Mahabharata war, during Krishna's final instructions to Uddhava before his departure.
story.comic_potential: The abstract philosophical dialogue may be challenging to depict visually, but could be illustrated through metaphors like the dream, the sun, and the gold ornament.

teachings.teaching: One should neither praise nor criticize the inherent nature and actions of others, seeing the entire universe as one with the Self, composed of prakriti and purusha.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the practice of non-judgmental awareness and equanimity, essential for inner stillness.

teachings.teaching: The world of duality is unreal like a dream; the self alone is real, and attachment to duality leads to suffering.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that through Kriya-Yoga, one directly experiences the dream-like nature of reality, leading to detachment.

teachings.teaching: The self is the sole reality; the universe is created, sustained, and dissolved by the self as the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga understanding that the individual self is identical with the cosmic self, realized through meditation.

teachings.teaching: The wise person, knowing the self, moves in the world like the sun, neither praising nor blaming others.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches the yogi to remain unaffected by external events, like the sun shining impartially.

teachings.teaching: Samsara exists only as long as there is identification with the body, senses, and mind; it is like a dream that ceases upon awakening.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, awakening is achieved through pranayama and meditation, which dissolve the illusion of separation.

teachings.teaching: The ego is the cause of all afflictions; the self is unaffected and pure.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that Kriya-Yoga purifies the ego, revealing the self as blissful consciousness.

teachings.teaching: The root of samsara is cut by the sword of knowledge sharpened by devotion.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes Sharma's emphasis on jnana (knowledge) and bhakti (devotion) as complementary paths in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: The self is like gold that pervades all ornaments; the world is a modification of the one reality.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma uses this analogy to explain that all forms are manifestations of the same divine essence, realized through Kriya.

teachings.teaching: The enlightened one, established in the self, does not identify with actions or the body.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the practitioner attains a state of witness consciousness, free from karmic bonds.

teachings.teaching: Obstacles in yoga can be overcome through asanas, pranayama, mantra, meditation on the Lord, and chanting His names.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga incorporates these methods as practical tools for purification and concentration.

teachings.teaching: The yogi should not be attached to the body, even if it becomes perfect; the goal is to transcend it through devotion to the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma warns against mistaking physical perfection for spiritual progress; the ultimate aim is union with the divine.

chapter_summary: Uddhava asks Krishna about the difficulty of yoga practice and the path to liberation. Krishna responds by teaching the essence of bhakti-yoga, emphasizing surrender, equal vision, and devotion to Him. He instructs Uddhava to go to Badarikashrama and practice detachment. Uddhava, overwhelmed with love, departs for the forest.

story.title: Uddhava's Inquiry and Krishna's Final Instructions
story.arc: Uddhava, feeling the difficulty of yoga, asks Krishna for an easier path. Krishna teaches the essence of bhakti-yoga, emphasizing surrender and equal vision. He then instructs Uddhava to go to Badarikashrama and practice detachment. Uddhava, though reluctant to leave, obeys and departs.
story.timeline_note: This is Krishna's final instruction to Uddhava before his departure to Badarikashrama, occurring after the main Bhagavata teachings.
story.comic_potential: Uddhava's emotional struggle at leaving Krishna, with tears and repeated prostrations, has dramatic and poignant potential.

teachings.teaching: Perform all actions for Krishna, remembering Him, and fix the mind on Him.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the practice of offering every action to the divine, cultivating constant remembrance.

teachings.teaching: See the same Lord in all beings, both inside and outside, like the sky.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga goal of realizing the unity of existence through inner stillness.

teachings.teaching: One who sees equally a brahmana, a dog-eater, a thief, and the sun is considered wise.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes transcending dualities through equal vision, a key Kriya-Yoga principle.

teachings.teaching: Prostrate like a stick before all beings, including a dog or an outcaste, until the sense of oneness arises.
teachings.lens_note: This practice of humility dissolves ego, a core Kriya-Yoga discipline.

teachings.teaching: When one surrenders all actions and offers oneself to Krishna, one attains immortality and becomes fit for union with Him.
teachings.lens_note: This is the culmination of Kriya-Yoga: complete surrender leading to liberation.

chapter_summary: Krishna orchestrates the destruction of the Yadu dynasty through a curse, leading to a fratricidal war at Prabhasa. After the Yadava annihilation, Balarama departs via yoga, and Krishna is accidentally shot by a hunter named Jara. Krishna then instructs his charioteer Daruka to inform the family and leaves his body, after which the city of Dvaraka is submerged by the ocean.

story.title: The End of the Yadava Dynasty and Krishna's Departure
story.arc: Climax of the Yadava subplot; Krishna's earthly mission concludes.
story.timeline_note: After the Mahabharata war, 36 years later, as per the curse of the brahmanas.
story.comic_potential: High; the fratricidal battle with eraka grass turning into weapons, the hunter Jara mistaking Krishna's foot for a deer, and the dramatic departure of Krishna.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's will is inscrutable; even devotees can be deluded by His maya.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that the ego (ahamkara) is the root of delusion; the Yadava warriors, though devotees, were overcome by pride and anger, leading to self-destruction. The teaching is to surrender the ego to the divine will.

teachings.teaching: One should not be attached to the body or material relationships; the Lord's presence is eternal.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the physical departure of Krishna is a lesson in non-attachment (vairagya). The true devotee sees Krishna's presence in all, not limited to a form.

teachings.teaching: The Lord forgives even the greatest sin if there is repentance and surrender.
teachings.lens_note: Jara's accidental killing of Krishna and subsequent forgiveness exemplifies the Kriya-Yoga principle of prapatti (total surrender). The Lord accepts the devotee's repentance and grants liberation.

teachings.teaching: The material world is transient; the Lord's abode is eternal.
teachings.lens_note: The submergence of Dvaraka symbolizes the impermanence of the material world. In Kriya-Yoga, this teaches the practitioner to seek the eternal self within, beyond the changing phenomena.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the departure of Krishna from the mortal world, his yoga-samadhi and return to his own abode, the reactions of various beings including Brahma and Shiva, the grief of the Vrishnis and Pandavas, the funeral rites, the submergence of Dvarka, and the installation of Vajra as king. It concludes with the benefits of reciting Krishna's deeds.

story.title: Krishna's Departure and Aftermath
story.arc: Krishna enters yoga-samadhi and leaves his mortal body, witnessed by gods and sages. The Vrishnis perish, Dvārakā is submerged, and Arjuna performs rites and installs Vajra as king.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the destruction of the Yadu dynasty and before the Pandavas' departure.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic departure of Krishna, the cosmic reactions, and the submergence of Dvārakā offer epic visual scenes.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's actions are like a magician's play; he creates, enters, and withdraws the universe by his own power.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the yogi's ability to withdraw from the world through inner absorption, realizing the self as the witness.

teachings.teaching: Even though Krishna has infinite power, he chose not to take his mortal body back to his abode, showing that the body is not the ultimate reality.
teachings.lens_note: This emphasizes the Kriya-Yoga teaching that the physical body is temporary and the goal is to transcend it through inner realization.

teachings.teaching: Reciting Krishna's deeds with faith frees one from all sins and bestows supreme devotion.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, such recitation is a form of bhakti that purifies the mind and leads to samadhi.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (12th Canto) presents a prophetic list of future kings and dynasties that will rule during the Kali Yuga, starting from the end of the Mahabharata war. It describes the decline of dharma and the rise of adharmic rulers, culminating in a chaotic period where mlecchas and shudras dominate.

story.title: Prophecy of Future Kings in Kali Yuga
story.arc: A prophetic narrative from Śuka to Parīkṣit, listing the dynasties that will rule after the Mahabharata war, culminating in the degradation of dharma.
story.timeline_note: Set in the Kali Yuga, after the Mahabharata war, covering thousands of years of future history.
story.comic_potential: A montage of successive kings and dynasties, with visual transitions showing the decline of righteousness and the rise of adharmic rulers.

teachings.teaching: The decline of dharma in Kali Yuga is inevitable, as kings become adharmic and society degenerates.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this underscores the urgency of inner purification through kriya-yoga to transcend the influence of the age.

teachings.teaching: The prophecy serves as a reminder of the cyclical nature of time and the importance of spiritual practice.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a call to focus on the eternal Self rather than being swayed by external circumstances.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the degradation of dharma in the Kali Yuga, the eventual appearance of Kalki avatar to restore righteousness, and the cyclical nature of the four yugas. It also lists the dynasties of kings and the duration of Kali Yuga.

story.title: The Degradation of Kali Yuga and the Advent of Kalki
story.arc: Prophetic description of the Kali Yuga's moral decline, the appearance of Kalki to restore dharma, and the cyclical return of the Satya Yuga.
story.timeline_note: Set in the future relative to the Mahabharata era, describing the Kali Yuga and its end.
story.comic_potential: High contrast between the degenerate Kali Yuga and the divine intervention of Kalki, with vivid imagery of chaos and restoration.

teachings.teaching: In Kali Yuga, wealth becomes the sole criterion for status, and dharma declines.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This reflects the externalization of spiritual values; true yoga is internal, not based on material wealth.

teachings.teaching: The Kali Yuga will be marked by greed, falsehood, and oppression, leading to suffering.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: The external chaos mirrors internal ignorance; Kriya-Yoga purifies the mind to overcome these tendencies.

teachings.teaching: Kalki will appear to destroy the wicked and re-establish dharma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: The advent of Kalki symbolizes the inner awakening that destroys ego and restores the soul's connection to the divine.

teachings.teaching: After Kalki's intervention, the Satya Yuga returns, and people's minds become pure.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This represents the culmination of spiritual practice, where the mind becomes transparent and aligned with the Self.

teachings.teaching: The cycle of four yugas repeats eternally.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: The yuga cycle mirrors the cycles of spiritual progress and regression in an individual's practice.

chapter_summary: The chapter describes the futility of worldly conquest and the inevitability of death, using the Earth's lament over kings who fight for her. It then details the characteristics of the four yugas (Krita, Treta, Dvapara, Kali) and emphasizes that in Kali Yuga, the only remedy is constant remembrance and chanting of Krishna's names, which purifies the heart and leads to liberation.

story.title: The Earth's Lament and the Glories of Kali-yuga
story.arc: Śuka describes the futility of material conquest and the characteristics of the yugas, culminating in the supreme remedy of chanting Krishna's names.
story.timeline_note: The chapter is set in the context of the Kali Yuga, with references to past kings and demons.
story.comic_potential: The Earth personified laughing at kings; vivid descriptions of Kali-yuga degradation; the dramatic contrast between futile wars and the simple path of chanting.

teachings.teaching: Worldly conquest is futile; all kings and demons eventually become mere stories.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the need to turn inward and conquer the inner enemies (six passions) rather than external territories.

teachings.teaching: The four yugas are characterized by the predominance of sattva, rajas, and tamas, and the gradual decline of dharma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees the yugas as internal states: Krita is pure consciousness, Treta is active devotion, Dvapara is ritualistic practice, Kali is the age of chanting as the primary sadhana.

teachings.teaching: In Kali Yuga, the only effective means of purification is constant remembrance and chanting of Krishna's names.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on nama-japa and internal meditation as the quickest path to self-realization in the current age.

teachings.teaching: The Lord residing in the heart removes all impurities, just as fire purifies gold.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the Kriya-Yoga process where the inner fire of meditation burns away karmic impurities.

chapter_summary: This verse compares the results of spiritual practices in different yugas: in Krita Yuga, meditation on Vishnu; in Treta Yuga, performing sacrifices; in Dvapara Yuga, worship through service; and in Kali Yuga, chanting the name of Hari (Krishna) yields the same result.

story.title: The Glory of Hari-Kirtana in Kali Yuga
story.arc: A single verse describing the efficacy of different spiritual practices across the four yugas, culminating in the supremacy of chanting Hari's name in Kali Yuga.
story.timeline_note: Refers to the four yugas: Krita, Treta, Dvapara, and Kali.
story.comic_potential: Minimal; it is a didactic statement rather than a narrative.

teachings.teaching: In Kali Yuga, chanting the name of Hari (Krishna) is equivalent to meditation, sacrifice, and worship performed in previous yugas.
teachings.lens_note: Emphasizes the power of nama-sankirtana as the recommended practice for the current age.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the four types of cosmic dissolution (pralaya): nitya (constant), naimittika (occasional), prākṛtika (elemental), and ātyantika (ultimate). It details the process of elemental dissolution, the nature of the ultimate reality beyond all guṇas, and the means to attain liberation through devotion to the Lord's pastimes. The chapter concludes with the lineage of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa's transmission.

story.title: The Four Types of Dissolution and the Ultimate Reality
story.arc: Cosmology and Liberation
story.timeline_note: The chapter describes cyclical time scales from atomic to para-ardha, and the four types of pralaya.
story.comic_potential: The vivid description of cosmic dissolution—fire, wind, rain, and elemental merging—could be depicted as a dramatic sequence of destruction and absorption.

teachings.teaching: The ultimate reality (Brahman) is beyond all guṇas, devoid of name, form, and mental constructs; it is the substratum of all.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the state of nirvikalpa samadhi where the practitioner transcends all modifications of mind and realizes the pure consciousness.

teachings.teaching: The world is a mere appearance (māyā) in the Self, like clouds appearing and disappearing in the sky.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that through sustained practice, one directly perceives the world as a projection of consciousness, leading to detachment.

teachings.teaching: Liberation (ātyantika pralaya) is achieved by cutting the knot of ego (ahaṅkāra) with the sword of discrimination and realizing one's identity with the Supreme.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga goal of dissolving the ego through pranayama and meditation, culminating in the experience of the Self as Brahman.

teachings.teaching: The only boat to cross the ocean of saṃsāra is the constant immersion in the pastimes of the Lord (līlā-kathā).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga incorporates devotional elements, teaching that hearing and chanting the Lord's glories purifies the mind and accelerates spiritual progress.

chapter_summary: Śrī Śuka instructs King Parīkṣit on the nature of the self, explaining that the ātman is eternal, unborn, and distinct from the body, using analogies like the dream, the pot-space, and the lamp. He urges the king to give up the animalistic notion of death and realize his identity with Brahman, the supreme reality, through meditation on Vāsudeva.

story.title: Śuka's Teaching on the Eternal Self
story.arc: Śrī Śuka instructs King Parīkṣit on the nature of the ātman, urging him to transcend the fear of death and realize his identity with Brahman.
story.timeline_note: This teaching occurs shortly before King Parīkṣit's death by snakebite, as a final instruction.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic contrast between the king's impending death and the serene, philosophical discourse creates a poignant scene.

teachings.teaching: The ātman is unborn, eternal, and not subject to death; the body is like a pot that breaks, but the space within remains.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the self as distinct from the body-mind complex, achieved through pratyāhāra and dhyāna.

teachings.teaching: The jīva merges into Brahman upon death, like the space of a broken pot merges with the universal space.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga goal of merging individual consciousness with cosmic consciousness through kumbhaka and meditation.

teachings.teaching: The mind creates bodies, qualities, and actions; māyā creates the cycle of saṃsāra.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes controlling the mind through Kriya to break the cycle of karma and rebirth.

teachings.teaching: The self is self-luminous, beyond the manifest and unmanifest, and is the support like space.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga practice of witnessing the inner light (jyoti) during meditation.

teachings.teaching: One should contemplate the self as Brahman, the supreme abode, and thus transcend death.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga uses mantra and breath control to realize 'Aham Brahmāsmi' in samādhi.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit, having heard the Purāṇa from Śuka, attains liberation and is bitten by Takṣaka. His son Janamejaya performs a snake sacrifice, but is stopped by Bṛhaspati. The chapter then describes the origin of the Vedas from Oṃkāra and their division by Vyāsa.

story.title: Parīkṣit's Liberation and the Snake Sacrifice
story.arc: Parīkṣit attains liberation after hearing the Purāṇa; he is bitten by Takṣaka; Janamejaya performs snake sacrifice but is stopped by Bṛhaspati; then the origin of Vedas is described.
story.timeline_note: After the events of the Mahābhārata, during the Kali Yuga.
story.comic_potential: The snake sacrifice and the intervention of Bṛhaspati have dramatic and visual potential.

teachings.teaching: One should tolerate insults and not harbor enmity.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the practice of equanimity and non-reactivity.

teachings.teaching: The supreme goal is the Viṣṇu-pada, attained by giving up ego and possessiveness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes transcending the 'I' and 'mine' through meditation.

teachings.teaching: The origin of sound and the Vedas from Oṃkāra, the subtle essence.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga practice of listening to the inner sound (nāda).

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the transmission of the Vedas from Vyasa to his disciples and the subsequent branching of Vedic recensions. It details how Paila, Vaisampayana, Jaimini, and Sumantu received the Rig, Yajur, Sama, and Atharva Vedas respectively, and how their disciples further divided the texts into various shakhas. The story of Yajnavalkya's conflict with his guru Vaisampayana and his subsequent reception of the Shukla Yajurveda from the Sun god is also narrated.

story.title: The Transmission of the Vedas and the Origin of Vedic Shakhas
story.arc: This chapter traces the lineage of Vedic teachers from Vyasa to various disciples, detailing how each Veda was divided into multiple recensions. It includes the story of Yajnavalkya's falling out with Vaisampayana and his receiving the Shukla Yajurveda from the Sun god.
story.timeline_note: The events occur after the Mahabharata war, during the Dvapara Yuga transition to Kali Yuga.
story.comic_potential: The conflict between Yajnavalkya and Vaisampayana, and Yajnavalkya's prayer to the Sun, have dramatic potential.

teachings.teaching: The Vedas are transmitted through a lineage of teachers and disciples, emphasizing the importance of guru-parampara.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this underscores the necessity of receiving knowledge through a living tradition and personal initiation.

teachings.teaching: Yajnavalkya's prayer to the Sun describes the Sun as the inner controller and the source of time and life, highlighting the Sun's role as a manifestation of the divine.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views the Sun as a symbol of the inner light of consciousness; meditation on the Sun represents turning inward to the self.

teachings.teaching: The Sun grants Yajnavalkya the Shukla Yajurveda, showing that sincere devotion and practice can lead to direct revelation.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on direct experience through sadhana rather than mere study.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the lineage of teachers of the Atharva Veda and the Puranas, enumerating the eighteen major Puranas and their characteristics according to the ten lakṣaṇas (characteristics) of a Purana as defined by the sages.

story.title: The Lineage of Vedic and Purāṇic Teachers and the Characteristics of Purāṇas
story.arc: The chapter traces the disciplic succession of Atharva Veda and Purāṇa teachers, defines the ten characteristics of a Purāṇa, and lists the eighteen major Purāṇas.
story.timeline_note: The chapter describes the transmission of knowledge from Vyāsa through his disciples and their disciples, spanning multiple generations.
story.comic_potential: The listing of names and texts could be visualized as a genealogical chart or a library of Purāṇas.

teachings.teaching: A Purāṇa is defined by ten characteristics: sarga (creation), visarga (secondary creation), vṛtti (maintenance), rakṣā (protection), antarāṇi (intermediate periods), vaṃśa (dynasties), vaṃśānucarita (histories of dynasties), saṃsthā (dissolution), hetu (cause), and apāśraya (ultimate refuge).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, these ten characteristics can be seen as mapping to the ten stages of spiritual evolution: from the initial impulse (sarga) to the final absorption in the Self (apāśraya).

teachings.teaching: The ultimate refuge (apāśraya) is Brahman, which underlies all states of consciousness (waking, dreaming, deep sleep) and is realized when the mind ceases its threefold activity.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes the stilling of the mind's vṛttis (modifications) to realize the Self. This verse directly echoes that: when the mind abandons the threefold activity (vṛtti-traya), one knows the Self and turns away from the body-identification.

teachings.teaching: The cause (hetu) of creation is the jīva (individual soul) associated with avidyā (ignorance) and karma.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, avidyā is the root cause of bondage; through kriya-yoga practice, one burns the seeds of karma and attains liberation.

chapter_summary: Mārkaṇḍeya's penance and victory over death, Indra's attempt to disturb him, and the appearance of Nara-Nārāyaṇa.

story.title: Mārkaṇḍeya's Vision of Nara-Nārāyaṇa
story.arc: Mārkaṇḍeya's penance, Indra's failed temptation, and the appearance of the divine sages.
story.timeline_note: After six manvantaras of penance, during the seventh manvantara.
story.comic_potential: Indra's clumsy attempt to disturb Mārkaṇḍeya with celestial dancers and Kāma's arrows, only to be foiled by the sage's tejas.

teachings.teaching: By steadfast devotion and penance, one can conquer death and attain divine vision.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the power of sustained practice (abhyāsa) and detachment (vairāgya) to overcome the cycle of birth and death.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's grace is attained through unwavering focus and surrender.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the appearance of Nara-Nārāyaṇa is a result of Mārkaṇḍeya's intense meditation, showing that the divine responds to sincere yogic effort.

teachings.teaching: The material world and its distractions are powerless against one who is established in the self.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's teaching that the yogi, through inner stillness, remains unaffected by external stimuli.

chapter_summary: Lord Nārāyaṇa, pleased with Mārkaṇḍeya's devotion, grants him a boon. Mārkaṇḍeya asks to see the Lord's māyā. The Lord agrees and disappears. Mārkaṇḍeya then experiences a cosmic deluge, wandering alone for eons. Eventually, he sees a baby in a banyan tree, who inhales him, revealing the entire universe within. The baby is the Lord, who then vanishes, and Mārkaṇḍeya finds himself back in his hermitage.

story.title: Mārkaṇḍeya's Vision of the Cosmic Baby
story.arc: Mārkaṇḍeya, after receiving a boon from Nārāyaṇa, experiences the Lord's māyā as a cosmic deluge, then sees the universe within a baby, realizing the Lord's omnipresence.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Mārkaṇḍeya's long penance; the deluge lasts for eons.
story.comic_potential: The baby inhaling the sage and revealing the cosmos inside is a visually striking, surreal scene.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's māyā is inscrutable; the universe is contained within Him.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that the entire creation is a projection of the divine, and the yogi, through inner absorption, can perceive the macrocosm within the microcosm of the self.

teachings.teaching: Devotion and penance lead to direct vision of the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that bhakti combined with disciplined practice (kriya) culminates in sākṣātkāra, direct realization.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is both immanent and transcendent, appearing as a baby yet containing all.
teachings.lens_note: This paradox reflects the Kriya-Yoga understanding of the divine as both the inner self (guhā-śaya) and the cosmic person.

chapter_summary: Sage Mārkaṇḍeya, after experiencing Viṣṇu's māyā, seeks refuge at His feet. Lord Śiva and Pārvatī visit him, and Śiva grants him boons including unflinching devotion to Viṣṇu, eternal fame, and knowledge. Mārkaṇḍeya praises Śiva and the supreme Lord. The chapter concludes with the glory of hearing this narrative.

story.title: Mārkaṇḍeya's Vision and Śiva's Blessing
story.arc: Mārkaṇḍeya, after experiencing Viṣṇu's māyā, surrenders to Him. Śiva and Pārvatī visit him, and Śiva grants him boons of devotion, knowledge, and eternal fame.
story.timeline_note: After Mārkaṇḍeya's vision of Viṣṇu's māyā (previous chapter), he returns to his hermitage and is visited by Śiva.
story.comic_potential: Śiva and Pārvatī's sudden appearance in Mārkaṇḍeya's meditation, with Śiva's fearsome form, could be depicted humorously as a divine surprise.

teachings.teaching: The supreme goal is unflinching devotion to the Lord, beyond even liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the ultimate aim of merging individual will with divine will through sustained practice.

teachings.teaching: Association with saints is the highest gain for humans.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes satsaṅga as a key practice for purifying the mind and accelerating spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: Brāhmaṇas (sages) purify even by sight, more than holy waters or deities.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the power of realized beings in Kriya-Yoga, where the guru's grace is paramount.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's māyā is inconceivable; even gods are deluded by it.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that māyā is the divine play that veils the true self; Kriya-Yoga helps pierce this veil.

teachings.teaching: Hearing and narrating this story destroys karmic accumulations.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the power of śravaṇa (hearing) in Kriya-Yoga as a means to purify the subconscious.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the cosmic form of the Lord (Virāṭ Puruṣa) and the detailed iconography of Viṣṇu, including his weapons, ornaments, and associates. It also lists the solar deities presiding over each month, their names, and functions, emphasizing the cyclical nature of time and the Lord's manifestation as the sun.

story.title: The Cosmic Form and Solar Deities
story.arc: Exposition of the Lord's universal form and the monthly solar deities
story.timeline_note: This chapter is part of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa's twelfth skandha, likely narrated by Sūta to Śaunaka and other sages at Naimiṣāraṇya.
story.comic_potential: The vivid description of the Virāṭ Puruṣa with cosmic body parts and the procession of monthly deities could be visually striking.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's cosmic form encompasses all elements of creation; meditation on this form leads to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the practice of visualizing the cosmic form (Virāṭ) as a means to transcend the individual ego and realize unity with the universal consciousness.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's iconography (weapons, ornaments) symbolizes his attributes and powers.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets these as internal yogic energies: the conch (pranava), discus (chakra of time), mace (prana), etc., which are activated through kriya practices.

teachings.teaching: The sun as the Lord's manifestation regulates time and seasons through various deities.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees the solar months as cycles of pranic energy; the listed deities represent different aspects of the sun's influence on the practitioner's subtle body.

teachings.teaching: Recitation of the Lord's names and forms in the morning purifies the mind and leads to self-realization.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes daily practice (sadhana) of japa and meditation on the Lord's form as a means to still the mind and attain inner peace.

chapter_summary: Sūta summarizes the entire Bhāgavata Purāṇa, listing its contents from creation to the life of Kṛṣṇa, and extols the glory of hearing and reciting the Lord's pastimes.

story.title: Summary of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa
story.arc: Sūta recites the contents of the Purāṇa to the sages, culminating in the glorification of hearing Kṛṣṇa's pastimes.
story.timeline_note: The chapter is a retrospective summary of the entire Purāṇa, not a sequential narrative.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a dense list of contents and philosophical praise.

teachings.teaching: Hearing and chanting the names and pastimes of the Lord purifies one from all sins.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the practice of nāma-japa as a means to still the mind and connect with the divine.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's glories dispel all sorrow and darkness, like the sun dispels clouds.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the transformative power of kriya-yoga to remove ignorance and suffering.

teachings.teaching: Only speech that glorifies the Lord is true and auspicious; other talk is false and inauspicious.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's emphasis on satsaṅga and the importance of engaging in spiritual discourse.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to the Lord surpasses even impersonal knowledge (naiṣkarmya) if devoid of devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga integrates action and devotion, emphasizing that bhakti is the highest path.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (12.12.053-069) extols the glories of Lord Vasudeva (Krishna) and the benefits of reciting or hearing the Purana. It emphasizes that constant remembrance of Krishna's lotus feet destroys impurities and bestows purity, devotion, knowledge, and detachment. The chapter also describes the rewards for different classes of people who study or hear the Purana, and concludes with a prayer to the Supreme Lord and a salutation to Vyasa's son (Suta Goswami).

story.title: The Glories of the Bhagavata Purana
story.arc: Concluding verses of the Bhagavata Purana, summarizing its benefits and praising the Lord.
story.timeline_note: After the narration of the Purana to King Parikshit.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily didactic and devotional.

teachings.teaching: Constant remembrance of Krishna's lotus feet destroys all impurities and bestows auspiciousness, purity of mind, devotion to the Supreme, knowledge, and detachment.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the practice of constant awareness of the divine (Ishvara-pranidhana) as a means to purify the mind and attain higher states of consciousness.

teachings.teaching: Hearing or reciting even a single verse of the Bhagavata Purana with faith purifies the soul.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes the transformative power of sacred sound (mantra) and the importance of śraddhā (faith) in the practice.

teachings.teaching: Different classes of people (brahmana, kshatriya, vaishya, shudra) attain specific benefits from studying the Purana: wisdom, sovereignty, wealth, and purification from sin.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle that spiritual practices are adapted to individual capacities (adhikari-bheda).

chapter_summary: This chapter enumerates the eighteen major Purāṇas and their verse counts, extols the Śrīmad Bhāgavatam as the supreme Purāṇa, describes its origin from Lord Viṣṇu to Brahmā, and concludes with prayers to the Lord and Śuka. It also mentions the benefits of reading and donating the Bhāgavatam.

story.title: The Glorification of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa
story.arc: Concluding praise of the Bhāgavata as the essence of all Purāṇas and Vedānta.
story.timeline_note: Post-Kurukshetra, during the recitation to Śaunaka and other sages in Naimiṣāraṇya.
story.comic_potential: The vivid imagery of the cosmic turtle (Kūrma) and the churning of the ocean could be visually striking.

teachings.teaching: The Śrīmad Bhāgavatam is the essence of all Vedānta and the supreme Purāṇa; hearing it with devotion leads to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the culmination of jñāna (knowledge), vairāgya (detachment), and bhakti (devotion) leading to naiṣkarmya (actionlessness) and liberation.

teachings.teaching: The Bhāgavata was first taught by the Lord to Brahmā, then passed down through Nārada, Kṛṣṇa, and Śuka to Parīkṣit.
teachings.lens_note: This lineage emphasizes the importance of guru-paramparā (disciplic succession) in transmitting spiritual knowledge, a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: Donating a Bhāgavata on the full moon of Bhādrapada with a golden lion leads to the supreme goal.
teachings.lens_note: This teaching underscores the value of dāna (charity) as a sāttvic act that purifies the mind, aligning with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on selfless action.
